1	\chapter{The Grassmannian over $\mathbb{Z}$: affine charts and gluing}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianCells}
3	
4	\section{Set-up: charts indexed by $d$-subsets}
5	\label{sec:gr_setup}
6	
7	Fix integers \(r \ge d \ge 1\). For a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M\) over a ring
8	and a subset \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\), write \(M_I\)
9	for the \emph{\(I\)-th minor} of \(M\): the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose
10	columns are those indexed by \(I\). We construct the Grassmannian scheme
11	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing one affine chart \(U^I\) for
12	each such \(I\). The construction is, conceptually, the quotient
13	\(\mathrm{GL}_{d,\mathbb{Z}} \backslash V\) of the scheme \(V\) of rank-\(d\)
14	matrices, but we never use the language of group-scheme actions: the charts and
15	their transition maps are elementary matrix algebra over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Taking
16	\(d = 1\) recovers \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,1) = \mathbb{P}^{r-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\).
17	
18	\begin{definition}
19	
20	\leanok
21	[Affine chart \(U^I\)]
22	  \label{def:gr_affine_chart}
23	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.affineChart}
24	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
25	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
26	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
27	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
28	  % "If $M$ is a $d\times r$-matrix, and
29	  %  $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with cardinality $\#(I)$ equal
30	  %  to $d$, the $I$ th minor $M_I$ of $M$ will
31	  %  mean the $d\times d$ minor of $M$ whose columns are indexed by
32	  %  $I$.
33	  %
34	  %  For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
35	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
36	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
37	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$.
38	  %  Let $\Z[X^I]$ denote the polynomial ring in the variables
39	  %  $x^I_{p,q}$, and let $U^I = \spec \Z[X^I]$, which is non-canonically
40	  %  isomorphic to the affine space $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$."
41	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
42	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). Let \(X^I\) denote the
43	  \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\)
44	  identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\) and whose remaining \(d(r - d)\) entries are
45	  independent indeterminates \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\),
46	  \(q \in \{1,\dots,r\} \setminus I\)) over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Write
47	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) for the polynomial ring in these \(d(r-d)\) variables and
48	  set
49	  \[
50	    U^I \;:=\; \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I].
51	  \]
52	  The choice of an ordering of the free entries gives a (non-canonical)
53	  isomorphism \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\); in particular
54	  \(U^I\) is a smooth affine \(\mathbb{Z}\)-scheme of relative dimension
55	  \(d(r-d)\). Intuitively a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-point of \(U^I\) is a \(d \times r\)
56	  matrix whose \(I\)-columns form the identity, i.e.\ a rank-\(d\) quotient of
57	  \(\mathbb{Z}^{r}\) trivialised on the coordinates \(I\).
58	\end{definition}
59	
60	\section{Transition maps and the cocycle condition}
61	\label{sec:gr_transition}
62	
63	\subsection*{Mathlib inputs for the transition map}
64	
65	The construction of \(\theta_{I,J}\) is a Cramer-inverse computation over the
66	localised chart ring, followed by one application of the universal property of a
67	localisation. We record the four Mathlib facts it rests on as dependency
68	anchors.
69	
70	\begin{lemma}
71	[Localised element is a unit]
72	  \label{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
73	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.algebraMap_isUnit}
74	  \mathlibok
75	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
76	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(x \in R\), and \(S\) a localisation of \(R\)
77	  away from \(x\) (i.e.\ \(S = R[1/x]\)). Then the image of \(x\) under the
78	  structure map \(R \to S\) is a unit of \(S\).
79	\end{lemma}
80	
81	\begin{lemma}
82	[Left Cramer inverse]
83	  \label{lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul}
84	  \lean{Matrix.nonsing_inv_mul}
85	  \mathlibok
86	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
87	  For a square matrix \(A\) over a commutative ring with \(\det A\) a unit, the
88	  nonsingular inverse \(A^{-1}\) satisfies \(A^{-1} A = 1\).
89	\end{lemma}
90	
91	\begin{lemma}
92	[Right Cramer inverse]
93	  \label{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
94	  \lean{Matrix.mul_nonsing_inv}
95	  \mathlibok
96	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
97	  For a square matrix \(A\) over a commutative ring with \(\det A\) a unit, the
98	  nonsingular inverse \(A^{-1}\) satisfies \(A A^{-1} = 1\).
99	\end{lemma}
100	
101	\begin{lemma}
102	[Universal property of an away-localisation]
103	  \label{lem:mathlib_away_lift}
104	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.lift}
105	  \mathlibok
106	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
107	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(x \in R\), and \(S = R[1/x]\) a localisation
108	  of \(R\) away from \(x\). Given any ring homomorphism \(g : R \to P\) that
109	  sends \(x\) to a unit of \(P\), there is a unique ring homomorphism
110	  \(S \to P\) extending \(g\) along \(R \to S\).
111	\end{lemma}
112	
113	\subsection*{The transition map by matrix algebra}
114	
115	We now build \(\theta_{I,J}\) in seven steps over the localised chart ring
116	\(R^I_J := \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\), splitting at the matrix-algebra seams. The
117	ambient chart ring \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) is the polynomial ring of
118	\cref{def:gr_affine_chart} in the \(d(r-d)\) free variables \(x^I_{p,q}\)
119	(\(p \in \{1,\dots,d\}\), \(q \notin I\)).
120	
121	\begin{definition}
122	
123	\leanok
124	[The universal matrix \(X^I\)]
125	  \label{def:gr_universal_matrix}
126	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMatrix}
127	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart}
128	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
129	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
130	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
131	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
132	  % "For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
133	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
134	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
135	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$."
136	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). With the chart ring
137	  %   `R^I := MvPolynomial (Fin d × {q : Fin r // q ∉ I}) ℤ`, define
138	  %   `universalMatrix d r I : Matrix (Fin d) (Fin r) R^I` whose entry at
139	  %   `(p, q)` is: the identity entry `if (orderIso I) p = q then 1 else 0`
140	  %   when `q ∈ I` (using the order isomorphism `Fin d ≃o I` from `#I = d`),
141	  %   and the indeterminate `MvPolynomial.X (p, ⟨q, hq⟩)` when `q ∉ I`.
142	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
143	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). The \emph{universal
144	  matrix} \(X^I\) is the \(d \times r\) matrix over the chart ring
145	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) whose \(I\)-minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\) identity
146	  \(1_{d\times d}\) and whose remaining entries are the free indeterminates
147	  \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\), \(q \notin I\)). It is the universal point of
148	  the chart \(U^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}): a ring map
149	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to A\) is the same as a \(d \times r\) matrix over \(A\)
150	  with identity \(I\)-minor.
151	\end{definition}

153	\begin{definition}
154	
155	\leanok
156	[The minor determinant \(P^I_J\)]
157	  \label{def:gr_minor_det}
158	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.minorDet}
159	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix}
160	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
161	  % affine patches" (read from
162	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
163	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
164	  % "For any $J\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(J) =d$, let
165	  %  $P^I_J = \det(X^I_J) \in \Z[X^I]$
166	  %  where $X^I_J$ is the $J$ th minor of $X^I$."
167	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). `minorDet d r I J : R^I` is the
168	  %   determinant of the `d × d` submatrix of `universalMatrix d r I` whose
169	  %   columns are indexed by `J`, reindexed to `Fin d` via the order
170	  %   isomorphism `Fin d ≃o J` coming from `#J = d`:

173	  For \(J \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(J) = d\), let \(X^I_J\) denote the
174	  \(J\)-th minor of the universal matrix \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix})
175	  -- the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose columns are those indexed by \(J\). Set
176	  \[

290	  \leanok
291	  Immediate from \cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit} together with
292	  \cref{lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul} (left identity) and
293	  \cref{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv} (right identity), both of which apply once
294	  \(\det(X^I_J)\) is known to be a unit.
295	\end{proof}
296	
297	\begin{definition}
298	
299	\leanok
300	[The image matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\)]
301	  \label{def:gr_image_matrix}
302	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix}
303	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, def:gr_universal_matrix}
304	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
305	  % affine patches" (read from
306	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
307	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
308	  % "The images of the variables
309	  %  $x^J_{p,q}$ are given by the entries of the matrix formula
310	  %  $\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J) ^{-1}\,X^I$."
311	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target).
312	  %   `imageMatrix d r I J : Matrix (Fin d) (Fin r) (Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J))`
313	  %   `:= universalMinorInv d r I J * (universalMatrix d r I).map (algebraMap _ _)`.
314	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
315	  Define the \(d \times r\) matrix over \(R^I_J\)
316	  \[
317	    M \;:=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I \;\in\; \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times r}(R^I_J),
318	  \]
319	  the product of the Cramer inverse (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}) with the
320	  universal matrix \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) base-changed to
321	  \(R^I_J\). Its entries are the prospective images of the indeterminates
322	  \(x^J_{p,q}\) under \(\theta_{I,J}\). Its \(J\)-minor is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_J =
323	  1_{d\times d}\) (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}), so \(M\) is a
324	  point of the chart \(U^J\); and its \(I\)-minor is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I =
325	  (X^I_J)^{-1}\).
326	\end{definition}
327	
328	\begin{definition}
329	
330	\leanok
331	[The pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to R^I_J\)]
332	  \label{def:gr_transition_pre}
333	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionPreMap}
334	  \uses{def:gr_image_matrix}
335	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
336	  % affine patches" (read from
337	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
338	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
339	  % "a ring homomorphism
340	  %  $\theta_{I,J} : \Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \to \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$ is
341	  %  defined as follows. The images of the variables
342	  %  $x^J_{p,q}$ are given by the entries of the matrix formula
343	  %  $\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J) ^{-1}\,X^I$."
344	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). Via the universal property of the
345	  %   polynomial chart ring `R^J := MvPolynomial (Fin d × {q : Fin r // q ∉ J}) ℤ`,
346	  %   `transitionPreMap d r I J : R^J →ₐ[ℤ] Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J)`
347	  %   `:= MvPolynomial.aeval (fun e => imageMatrix d r I J e.1 e.2.1)`, i.e. the
348	  %   free indeterminate `x^J_{p,q}` is sent to the `(p,q)`-entry of `imageMatrix`.
349	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
350	  Let \(\tilde\theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to R^I_J\) be the
351	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism out of the chart ring of \(J\) determined,
352	  via the universal property of the polynomial ring, by sending each free
353	  indeterminate \(x^J_{p,q}\) to the \((p,q)\)-entry of the image matrix \(M =
354	  (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_image_matrix}); equivalently
355	  \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) as a matrix identity (the
356	  identity \(J\)-block of \(X^J\) matching the identity \(J\)-minor of \(M\)).
357	\end{definition}
358	
359	\begin{lemma}
360	
361	\leanok
362	[The pre-hom sends \(P^J_I\) to a unit]
363	  \label{lem:gr_transition_pre_unit}
364	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_transitionPreMap_minorDet}
365	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_minorDet_unit}
366	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
367	  % affine patches" (read from
368	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
369	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
370	  % "In particular, we have $\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = 1/P^I_J$,
371	  %  so the map extends to $\Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I]$."
372	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target).
373	  %   `isUnit_transitionPreMap_minorDet d r I J :`
374	  %   `IsUnit (transitionPreMap d r I J (minorDet d r J I))`.
375	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
376	  The pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre})
377	  sends \(P^J_I\) (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}, the \(I\)-minor determinant of
378	  \(X^J\)) to a unit of \(R^I_J\). Indeed
379	  \[
380	    \tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I\bigr)
381	      = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = \det(X^I_J)^{-1} = 1/P^I_J,
382	  \]
383	  which is a unit.
384	\end{lemma}
385	\begin{proof}
386	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_minorDet_unit}
387	  \leanok
388	  By \cref{def:gr_transition_pre}, \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = M = (X^I_J)^{-1}
389	  X^I\), so \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) carries the \(I\)-minor \(X^J_I\) of \(X^J\) to
390	  the \(I\)-minor of \(M\), which is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) since
391	  the \(I\)-minor of \(X^I\) is the identity. Hence \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) =
392	  \det((X^I_J)^{-1}) = \det(X^I_J)^{-1}\). As \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit
393	  (\cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}), so is its inverse; the matrix-inverse
394	  determinant identity uses \cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}.
395	\end{proof}
396	
397	\begin{definition}
398	
399	\leanok
400	[Transition map \(\theta_{I,J}\)]
401	  \label{def:gr_transition}
402	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionMap}
403	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, lem:gr_transition_pre_unit, lem:mathlib_away_lift, def:gr_affine_chart}
404	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
405	  % affine patches" (read from
406	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
407	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
408	  % "For any $I$ and $J$, a ring homomorphism
409	  %  $\theta_{I,J} : \Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \to \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$ is
410	  %  defined as follows. ...
411	  %  In particular, we have $\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = 1/P^I_J$,
412	  %  so the map extends to $\Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I]$."
413	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). The away-localisation lift of the
414	  %   pre-hom along `P^J_I`:
415	  %   `transitionMap d r I J :`
416	  %   `Localization.Away (minorDet d r J I) →+* Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J)`
417	  %   `:= IsLocalization.Away.lift (minorDet d r J I)`
418	  %   `(isUnit_transitionPreMap_minorDet d r I J)` applied to
419	  %   `(transitionPreMap d r I J : R^J →+* R^I_J)`.
420	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
421	  Because the pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to
422	  R^I_J\) (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}) sends \(P^J_I\) to a unit of \(R^I_J\)
423	  (\cref{lem:gr_transition_pre_unit}), the universal property of the
424	  away-localisation (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) provides a unique ring
425	  homomorphism extending it along \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to \mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I]\).
426	  This extension is the \emph{transition map}
427	  \[
428	    \theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \longrightarrow
429	    \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J], \qquad \theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I .
430	  \]
431	  Dually \(\theta_{I,J}\) is the comorphism of a morphism of schemes
432	  \(U^I_J \to U^J_I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}), which we also denote
433	  \(\theta_{I,J}\); it sends the universal matrix \(X^I\) to \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\).
434	\end{definition}
435	
436	\begin{lemma}
437	
438	\leanok
439	[\(\theta_{I,I}\) is the identity]
440	  \label{lem:gr_transition_self}
441	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionMap_self}
442	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_minor_det}
443	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
444	  % affine patches" (read from
445	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L838-L848).
446	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
447	  % "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$".
448	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target).
449	  %   `transitionMap_self d r I : transitionMap d r I I = RingHom.id _`
450	  %   (up to the identification `R^I_I = R^I` from `P^I_I = 1`).
451	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
452	  For every \(I\), \(\theta_{I,I} = \id_{U^I}\): since \(P^I_I = 1\)
453	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}) the minor \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\) is its own
454	  inverse, so \(\theta_{I,I}(X^I) = (X^I_I)^{-1} X^I = X^I\), i.e.\
455	  \(\theta_{I,I}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}) is the identity ring homomorphism
456	  of \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_I] = \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\).
457	\end{lemma}
458	\begin{proof}
459	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_minor_det}
460	  \leanok
461	  When \(J = I\) one has \(P^I_I = \det(1_{d\times d}) = 1\)
462	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}), so \(R^I_I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\), the minor \(X^I_I\)
463	  is the identity, and its Cramer inverse is again the identity. Hence the image
464	  matrix is \(M = 1 \cdot X^I = X^I\), the pre-hom sends each \(x^I_{p,q}\) to
465	  itself, and its localisation lift (\cref{def:gr_transition}) is the identity.
466	\end{proof}
467	
468	\subsection*{Project-local matrix and minor bookkeeping}
469	
470	The cocycle computation rests on a handful of elementary matrix-algebra
471	identities over the (localised) chart rings. They are project-internal helpers
472	with no external source; each is verified directly in Lean.
473	
474	\begin{lemma}
475	
476	\leanok
477	[Column submatrix of a product]
478	  \label{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}
479	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.mul_submatrix_col}
480	  Let \(A \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times d}(R)\) and
481	  \(B \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times r}(R)\) be matrices over a commutative ring,
482	  and \(g : \{1,\dots,d\} \to \{1,\dots,r\}\) a column-index map. Then taking the
483	  column submatrix of the product equals multiplying by the column submatrix of
484	  the right factor:
485	  \[
486	    (A B)_{\bullet\, g} \;=\; A \,\bigl(B_{\bullet\, g}\bigr).
487	  \]
488	\end{lemma}
489	\begin{proof}
490	
491	\leanok
492	  Entrywise from the definition of matrix multiplication (reindexing the rows by
493	  the identity leaves the contraction index untouched). Proved directly in Lean.
494	\end{proof}
495	
496	\begin{lemma}
497	
498	\leanok
499	[A ring map commutes with the Cramer inverse]
500	  \label{lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv}
501	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.map_nonsing_inv}
502	  \uses{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
503	  Let \(f : R \to S\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and
504	  \(A \in \operatorname{Mat}_{n\times n}(R)\) a square matrix with \(\det A\) a
505	  unit. Writing \(f_\ast\) for the entrywise application of \(f\), the
506	  nonsingular inverse is natural:
507	  \[
508	    \bigl(f_\ast A\bigr)^{-1} \;=\; f_\ast\bigl(A^{-1}\bigr).
509	  \]
510	\end{lemma}
511	\begin{proof}
512	
513	\leanok
514	  Since \(f_\ast A \cdot f_\ast(A^{-1}) = f_\ast(A A^{-1}) = f_\ast(1_{n\times n})
515	  = 1_{n\times n}\) (using \(A A^{-1} = 1\), \cref{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}),
516	  the matrix \(f_\ast(A^{-1})\) is a right inverse of \(f_\ast A\), hence equals
517	  \((f_\ast A)^{-1}\). Proved directly in Lean.
518	\end{proof}
519	
520	\begin{lemma}
521	
522	\leanok
523	[Functoriality of entrywise application]
524	  \label{lem:gr_map_map_eq_of_comp}
525	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.map_map_eq_of_comp}
526	  Let \(f : R \to A\), \(g : A \to D\) and \(h : R \to D\) be ring
527	  homomorphisms with \(g \circ f = h\). Then for any matrix \(M\) over \(R\),
528	  applying \(f\) then \(g\) entrywise equals applying \(h\) entrywise:
529	  \(g_\ast(f_\ast M) = h_\ast M\).
530	\end{lemma}
531	\begin{proof}
532	
533	\leanok
534	  Immediate from functoriality of \(\operatorname{Mat}(-)\) (composition of
535	  entrywise maps). Proved directly in Lean.
536	\end{proof}
537	
538	\begin{lemma}
539	
540	\leanok
541	[Inverse cancellation in a composite]
542	  \label{lem:gr_inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel}
543	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel}
544	  \uses{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
545	  Let \(A, B \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times d}(R)\) and
546	  \(M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times e}(R)\) over a commutative ring, with
547	  \(\det A\) a unit. Then
548	  \[
549	    \bigl(B^{-1} A\bigr)\,\bigl(A^{-1} M\bigr) \;=\; B^{-1} M.
550	  \]
551	\end{lemma}
552	\begin{proof}
553	
554	\leanok
555	  By associativity and \(A A^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}\)
556	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}). Proved directly in Lean.
557	\end{proof}
558	
559	\begin{lemma}
560	
561	\leanok
562	[The \(I\)-minor of \(X^I\) is the identity]
563	  \label{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
564	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
565	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix}
566	  The \(I\)-minor of the universal matrix \(X^I\)
567	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}), with its \(I\)-columns read through the order
568	  isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \xrightarrow{\sim} I\), is the \(d\times d\)
569	  identity: \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\). (This is the normalisation \(P^I_I = 1\)
570	  defining the chart.)
571	\end{lemma}
572	\begin{proof}
573	
574	\leanok
575	  By \cref{def:gr_universal_matrix} the \(q\)-column with \(q \in I\) is the
576	  standard basis vector \(e_p\) where \(p\) is the preimage of \(q\) under the
577	  order isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \xrightarrow{\sim} I\); so the \(I\)-minor
578	  is the identity. Proved directly in Lean.
579	\end{proof}
580	
581	\begin{lemma}
582	
583	\leanok
584	[The \(J\)-minor of the image matrix is the identity]
585	  \label{lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_self}
586	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix_submatrix_self}
587	  \uses{def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}
588	  The \(J\)-minor of the image matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\)
589	  (\cref{def:gr_image_matrix}) is the identity:
590	  \[
591	    M_J \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I_J \;=\; 1_{d\times d}.
592	  \]
593	\end{lemma}
594	\begin{proof}
595	
596	\leanok
597	  The \(J\)-column submatrix of \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is
598	  \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) times the \(J\)-column submatrix of \(X^I\), i.e.
599	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_J\), by \cref{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}; this is
600	  \(1_{d\times d}\) by the left-inverse identity
601	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}). Proved directly in Lean.
602	\end{proof}
603	
604	\begin{lemma}
605	
606	\leanok
607	[The \(I\)-minor of the image matrix is the Cramer inverse]
608	  \label{lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_I}
609	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix_submatrix_I}
610	  \uses{def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col, lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
611	  The \(I\)-minor of the image matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\)
612	  (\cref{def:gr_image_matrix}) is the Cramer inverse:
613	  \[
614	    M_I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I_I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}.
615	  \]
616	\end{lemma}
617	\begin{proof}
618	
619	\leanok
620	  By \cref{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col} the \(I\)-column submatrix of \(M\) is
621	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I\), and \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\)
622	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}). Proved directly in Lean.
623	\end{proof}
624	
625	\begin{lemma}
626	
627	\leanok
628	[\(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) realises the matrix formula]
629	  \label{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap}
630	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap}
631	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_image_matrix, def:gr_transition_pre, lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_self}
632	  Applying the pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\)
633	  (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}) entrywise to the universal matrix \(X^J\) yields
634	  the image matrix:
635	  \[
636	    (\tilde\theta_{I,J})_\ast\, X^J \;=\; M \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I .
637	  \]
638	\end{lemma}
639	\begin{proof}
640	
641	\leanok
642	  On a free entry \(x^J_{p,q}\) (\(q \notin J\)) this holds by the definition of
643	  \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) as evaluation at the entries of \(M\)
644	  (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}). On a column \(q \in J\) the entry of \(X^J\) is
645	  an identity-block entry, which \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) carries to the
646	  corresponding entry of \(M_J = 1_{d\times d}\)
647	  (\cref{lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_self}). Proved directly in Lean.
648	\end{proof}
649	
650	\begin{lemma}
651	
652	\leanok
653	[Image of a cross minor under \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\)]
654	  \label{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet}
655	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionPreMap_minorDet}
656	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap}
657	  For a third size-\(d\) subset \(K\), the pre-hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) sends the
658	  minor determinant \(P^J_K\) (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}) to the determinant of the
659	  \(K\)-minor of the image matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\):
660	  \[
661	    \tilde\theta_{I,J}\bigl(P^J_K\bigr) \;=\; \det\bigl(M_K\bigr).
662	  \]
663	\end{lemma}
664	\begin{proof}
665	
666	\leanok
667	  The hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) commutes with determinants and with passing to
668	  a column submatrix, so \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_K) =
669	  \det\bigl((\tilde\theta_{I,J})_\ast X^J\bigr)_K\); apply
670	  \cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap}. Proved directly in Lean.
671	\end{proof}
672	
673	\begin{lemma}
674	
675	\leanok
676	[Naturality of the image matrix]
677	  \label{lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_eq}
678	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix_map_eq}
679	  \uses{def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_map_map_eq_of_comp, def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv, lem:gr_minorDet_unit, def:gr_universal_minor}
680	  Let \(X\) be a size-\(d\) subset and \(\iota : R^I_X \to D\) a ring
681	  homomorphism lying over the structure map \(R^I \to D\), i.e.\
682	  \(\iota \circ (R^I \to R^I_X) = (R^I \to D)\). Writing \(Y\) for the universal
683	  matrix \(X^I\) base-changed to \(D\) and \(Y_X\) for its \(X\)-minor, one has
684	  \[
685	    \iota_\ast\bigl((X^I_X)^{-1} X^I\bigr) \;=\; (Y_X)^{-1}\, Y .
686	  \]
687	\end{lemma}
688	\begin{proof}
689	
690	\leanok
691	  Distribute \(\iota_\ast\) across the product \(M = (X^I_X)^{-1} X^I\); the
692	  factor \((X^I_X)^{-1}\) passes through \(\iota_\ast\) as a Cramer inverse
693	  (\cref{lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv}, using \cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}), and both
694	  factors collapse to base changes over \(D\) by functoriality
695	  (\cref{lem:gr_map_map_eq_of_comp}) since \(\iota\) lies over \(R^I \to D\).
696	  Proved directly in Lean.
697	\end{proof}

699	\subsection*{Triple-overlap rings and the localised transition maps}
700	
701	The cocycle condition \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\) cannot
702	be stated as a naive composition of the landed transition maps of
703	\cref{def:gr_transition}: the codomain of \(\theta_{J,K}\) (the ring
704	\(R^J[1/P^J_K]\)) differs from the domain of \(\theta_{I,J}\) (the ring
705	\(R^J[1/P^J_I]\)). The identity therefore lives over the \emph{triple-overlap}
706	rings obtained by inverting both relevant minors in each chart ring:
707	\[
708	  S_K := R^K[1/(P^K_I P^K_J)], \quad
709	  S_J := R^J[1/(P^J_I P^J_K)], \quad
710	  S_I := R^I[1/(P^I_J P^I_K)].
711	\]
712	
713	\begin{definition}
714	
715	\leanok

717	  \label{def:gr_away_incl_left}

719	  \uses{lem:mathlib_away_lift, lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
720	  For \(x, y \in R\), the canonical inclusion
721	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}}_{x,y} : R[1/x] \to R[1/(xy)]\) inverting the extra factor
722	  \(y\), defined as the away-localisation lift (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) of
723	  the structure map \(R \to R[1/(xy)]\) along \(x\) — well-defined because \(x\)
724	  is a unit of \(R[1/(xy)]\) (a factor of the unit \(xy\),
725	  \cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}).
726	\end{definition}

898	[Image-matrix identity over the triple overlap]
899	  \label{lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq}

901	  \uses{lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_eq, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_away_incl_right, def:gr_away_incl_left, lem:gr_awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap, lem:gr_awayInclLeft_comp_algebraMap, lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap, lem:gr_map_map_eq_of_comp, lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col, lem:gr_inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel, lem:gr_isUnit_algebraMap_away_left, lem:gr_minorDet_unit, def:gr_image_matrix, def:gr_universal_minor}
902	  Over the triple-overlap ring \(S_I\), the image matrix of \(\theta_{I,K}\)
903	  equals \(\Theta_{I,J}\) applied entrywise to the image matrix of
904	  \(\theta_{J,K}\):
905	  \[
906	    \iota^{\mathrm{R}}_\ast\bigl((X^I_K)^{-1} X^I\bigr)
907	      \;=\;
908	      \bigl(\Theta_{I,J}\circ\iota^{\mathrm{R}}\bigr)_\ast
909	        \bigl((X^J_K)^{-1} X^J\bigr) .
910	  \]

912	  \(X^I\) base-changed to \(S_I\) and \(Y_K\) its \(K\)-minor.

918	  (\cref{lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_eq}, via \cref{lem:gr_awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap}).
919	  For the right side, \(\Theta_{I,J}\circ\iota^{\mathrm{R}}\) carries the chart
920	  ring \(R^J\) over \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}}\circ\tilde\theta_{I,J}\), so the image
921	  matrix of \(\theta_{J,K}\) maps to \((M^J_K)^{-1} M^J\) with
922	  \(M^J = (Y_J)^{-1} Y\) (\cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap},

925	  the cancellation \((Y_K)^{-1} Y_J \cdot (Y_J)^{-1} Y = (Y_K)^{-1} Y\)
926	  (\cref{lem:gr_inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel}, using \(\det Y_J\) a unit by

931	\begin{lemma}
932	
933	\leanok
934	[Cocycle condition for the transition maps]
935	  \label{lem:gr_cocycle}
936	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.cocycleCondition}
937	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_transition_self, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_cocycle_theta_jk, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ik, lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq}
938	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
939	  % affine patches" (read from
940	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L838-L848).
941	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
942	  % "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$, and
943	  %  we leave it to the reader to verify that
944	  %  for any three subsets $I$, $J$ and $K$ of $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of
945	  %  cardinality $d$, the co-cycle condition
946	  %  $\theta_{I,K}= \theta_{I,J}\theta_{J,K}$
947	  %  is satisfied."
948	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). Composition direction: the
949	  %   RING-HOM identity `θ_{I,K} = θ_{I,J} ∘ θ_{J,K}` (the scheme-morphism reading
950	  %   `θ_{J,K} ∘ θ_{I,J}` is its Spec-dual). Recall the landed orientation
951	  %   `transitionMap d r I J hI hJ :`
952	  %     `Localization.Away (minorDet d r J I hJ hI) →+* Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J hI hJ)`
953	  %   (domain R^J[1/P^J_I], codomain R^I[1/P^I_J]).
954	  %   Note: the naive
955	  %     `transitionMap d r I K hI hK = (transitionMap d r I J hI hJ).comp (transitionMap d r J K hJ hK)`
956	  %   does NOT typecheck. `transitionMap d r J K` has codomain
957	  %   `Localization.Away (minorDet d r J K hJ hK)` (R^J[1/P^J_K]) while
958	  %   `transitionMap d r I J` has domain
959	  %   `Localization.Away (minorDet d r J I hJ hI)` (R^J[1/P^J_I]); likewise the
960	  %   domain of `transitionMap d r I K` is `Localization.Away (minorDet d r K I hK hI)`
961	  %   (R^K[1/P^K_I]) but the composite would start from
962	  %   `Localization.Away (minorDet d r K J hK hJ)` (R^K[1/P^K_J]). The two
963	  %   `IsLocalization.Away.lift` codomains/domains differ, so the identity lives over
964	  %   the TRIPLE-OVERLAP rings obtained by inverting BOTH relevant minors:
965	  %     S_K := Localization.Away (minorDet d r K I hK hI * minorDet d r K J hK hJ)  -- R^K[1/(P^K_I P^K_J)]
966	  %     S_J := Localization.Away (minorDet d r J I hJ hI * minorDet d r J K hJ hK)  -- R^J[1/(P^J_I P^J_K)]
967	  %     S_I := Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J hI hJ * minorDet d r I K hI hK)  -- R^I[1/(P^I_J P^I_K)]
968	  %   With the canonical `algebraMap`s from each single away-localisation into the
969	  %   matching double one, the localised transition maps
970	  %     Θ_{J,K} : S_K →+* S_J,  Θ_{I,J} : S_J →+* S_I,  Θ_{I,K} : S_K →+* S_I
971	  %   are the `IsLocalization.Away.lift`s (equivalently the `IsLocalization.lift`s of
972	  %   `transitionMap …` along the extra inverted minor) of the respective
973	  %   `transitionPreMap` composed with the structure map into the double localisation.
974	  %   The conclusion is the propositional equality of ring homs `S_K →+* S_I`:
975	  %     `cocycleCondition d r I J K (hI : I.card = d) (hJ : J.card = d) (hK : K.card = d) :`
976	  %       `Θ_{I,K} = Θ_{I,J}.comp Θ_{J,K}`.
977	  %   (It is acceptable to pin the conclusion over the chart ring S_I on the
978	  %   triple-overlap open, as the prose proof does; the doubly-localised rings
979	  %   S_K, S_J, S_I are exactly that open's coordinate rings.)
980	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
981	  For any three subsets \(I, J, K \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) of cardinality
982	  \(d\), the transition maps of \cref{def:gr_transition} satisfy the cocycle
983	  condition. As morphisms of schemes, on the triple overlap inside \(U^I\),
984	  \[
985	    \theta_{I,K} \;=\; \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J},
986	  \]
987	  equivalently, as ring homomorphisms,
988	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\) (the two read the same
989	  identity through the order-reversal of \(\Spec\)). Together with
990	  \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\), this is exactly the compatibility datum required to
991	  glue the charts.
992	\end{lemma}
993	
994	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$, and
995	% we leave it to the reader to verify that
996	% for any three subsets $I$, $J$ and $K$ of $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of
997	% cardinality $d$, the co-cycle condition
998	% $\theta_{I,K}= \theta_{I,J}\theta_{J,K}$
999	% is satisfied."  (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
1000	% L838-L848.)
1001	\begin{proof}
1002	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_transition_self, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_cocycle_theta_jk, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ik, lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq}
1003	  \leanok
1004	  We verify the scheme-morphism form
1005	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) by a direct matrix
1006	  computation, valid over the open locus of \(U^I\) on which all the minors
1007	  involved are invertible (namely \(P^I_J\) and \(P^I_K\), which suffices since
1008	  the maps are determined by their restriction to a dense open and all rings are
1009	  domains; the computation is an identity of matrices with entries in
1010	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J, 1/P^I_K]\)).
1011	
1012	  Start from the matrix \(X^I\) (the universal point of \(U^I\)). By
1013	  \cref{def:gr_transition},
1014	  \[
1015	    M^J \;:=\; \theta_{I,J}(X^I) \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I,
1016	  \]
1017	  a \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(J\)-minor is the identity, hence a point of
1018	  \(U^J\). Its \(K\)-minor is
1019	  \[
1020	    M^J_K \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_K,
1021	    \qquad\text{so}\qquad
1022	    (M^J_K)^{-1} \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I_J,
1023	  \]
1024	  using \((AB)^{-1} = B^{-1} A^{-1}\) for the invertible \(d \times d\) matrices
1025	  \(X^I_J\) and \(X^I_K\). Applying \(\theta_{J,K}\) to \(M^J\) gives
1026	  \[
1027	    \theta_{J,K}(M^J) \;=\; (M^J_K)^{-1} M^J
1028	    \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I_J \,\cdot\, (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I
1029	    \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I,
1030	  \]
1031	  the middle product \(X^I_J (X^I_J)^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}\) cancelling by
1032	  associativity of matrix multiplication. The right-hand side is precisely
1033	  \(\theta_{I,K}(X^I) = (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I\). Thus
1034	  \(\theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) and \(\theta_{I,K}\) agree on the
1035	  universal matrix \(X^I\), hence agree as morphisms \(U^I \supseteq U^I_J \cap
1036	  U^I_K \to U^K\). The special case \(K = I\) (resp.\ \(J = I\)) together with
1037	  \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\) shows each \(\theta_{I,J}\) is an isomorphism onto its
1038	  image with inverse \(\theta_{J,I}\), since
1039	  \(\theta_{J,I} \circ \theta_{I,J} = \theta_{I,I} = \id\).
1040	\end{proof}

1042	\section{Scheme-level glue-data layer}

2268	  whole four-factor chain — for \emph{generic} morphisms \(g', f'\) carrying the cone-leg

2294	  surviving non-trivial factor is \(f_{*}\bigl(\eta^{g'}_{\widetilde M}\bigr)\) read on global

2576	  \((\operatorname{pr}_1, \operatorname{pr}_2)\) by Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}.
2577	  Distributing first keeps each of the four factors in the free \(a\!\circ\!b\) shape against which the
2578	  three atoms are stated, so they apply directly. If instead one first reads through the codomain
2579	  identification (substituting the literal projection legs and collapsing the
2580	  \(\mathrm{pushforwardComp}\) bookkeeping), the surviving \((g')\)-unit becomes the rigid literal
2581	  \((\mathrm{pullbackSpecIso})^{\mathrm{hom}} \circ \operatorname{Spec}(\mathrm{includeLeft})\), whose
2582	  shape no longer presents as an \((a\!\circ\!b)\)-unit and against which the atoms are inapplicable.
2583	  Hence: distribute the unit on the free composite first; cancel with the three atoms; \emph{then}
2584	  read through Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}.
2585	\end{proof}
2586

2588	
2589

2591	[(A-2$'$) Assemble the eCancel telescoping in the leg-parametrised form]
2592	  \label{lem:base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_assemble}
2593	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_mate_fstar_reindex_legs}
2594	  % NOTE: superseded by the conjugate-side re-encoding (Open Q2). This was the LIVE realization of the
2595	  % eCancel telescoping inside `_legs` under the abandoned direct-on-sections route; the live `_legs`
2596	  % proof is now the conjugate-component identity, which does not route through this telescoping. The
2597	  % consuming lemmas (gstar_transpose) no longer `\uses` it. Lean pin (the subsuming `_legs` theorem)
2598	  % retained.

2600	  % link 3); the \lean{} pin names that subsuming theorem. It is the leg-parametrised analogue of
2601	  % the inline eCancel: the same three one-cancellation atoms, run against the leg-parametrised
2602	  % codomain read, where the leg identification \(g' = e \circ \mathrm{Spec}\,\iota_A\) is
2603	  % definitional, so each atom's factor matches and the cancellation proceeds.
2604	  % NOTE (mechanism): making the legs definitional removes the dependent-motive wall of the
2605	  % literal-leg form, but a SECOND diamond survives — the nested-image vs composed-functor object
2606	  % diamond on the `X.Modules` `CategoryStruct.comp`/`Functor.map` instance. After Γ is distributed
2607	  % over the composite the factor to collapse sits over the nested-image object `G.obj (H.obj M)`,
2608	  % while the collapsing atom fixes the composed-functor object `(H ⋙ G).obj M`; these are defeq but
2609	  % not syntactic. Each factor-equation is lifted into the ambient composite one neighbour at a time by

2611	  \uses{lem:base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_eUnit,
2612	        lem:base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_pushforwardComp,
2613	        lem:base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_pullbackComp,

2617	  In the leg-parametrised reindex Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex_legs}, after the
2618	  unit expansion and four-factor \(\Gamma\)-distribution (step~(iii), links~(1)--(2)), the four
2619	  distributed \(\Gamma\)-image factors cancel against the unfolded leg-parametrised codomain read
2620	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read_legs}, in the order:
2621	  \begin{enumerate}

2624	      built into the codomain read;

2627	      \(\Gamma\)-image (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_pushforwardComp}; the
2628	      unwrapped factor by Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaMap_pushforwardComp_hom_eq_id}) and drops;
2629	    \item the trailing

2637	  Seam~1 (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_unit_value}). This is the residual telescoping that closes
2638	  the leg-parametrised reindex; it is the single remaining target of Seam~A.

2641	  \uses{lem:base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_eUnit,
2642	        lem:base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_pushforwardComp,
2643	        lem:base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_pullbackComp,

2648	  codomain read Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read_legs} by the three one-cancellation
2649	  atoms. Carrying the legs as free parameters and identifying them with
2650	  \(e \circ \operatorname{Spec}\iota_A\) definitionally removes the dependent-motive obstruction of
2651	  the literal-leg form.
2652	
2653	  \emph{The residual object-form diamond.} After distributing \(\Gamma\) over the composite, the
2654	  factor to be collapsed sits over the \emph{nested-image} object \(G(H(M))\) — the form produced
2655	  by distributing a functor over a composite of morphisms — whereas the collapsing atom fixes the
2656	  same object in its \emph{composed-functor} form \((H \circ G)(M)\), the form in which the
2657	  pushforward-composition coherence isomorphism presents its (co)domain. These two object
2658	  presentations are definitionally but not syntactically equal, creating a diamond that recurs at
2659	  each of the three cancellations.
2660	
2661	  \emph{The resolution: congruence lifting.} Each factor-equation is lifted into the ambient
2662	  composite \emph{one neighbour at a time}: a factor identity \(u = u'\) is promoted to
2663	  \(c \circ u = c \circ u'\), or to \(u \circ c = u' \circ c\), by congruence in the composition
2664	  operation with the neighbouring factor \(c\) held fixed, and the promoted steps are chained in
2665	  sequence. The chain is then closed on the definitional bridge between the nested-image and
2666	  composed-functor presentations of the shared object, absorbing the object-form diamond at that
2667	  single closing step. The three atoms
2668	  (Lemmas~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_eUnit},
2669	  \ref{lem:base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_pushforwardComp},
2670	  \ref{lem:base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_pullbackComp}) are supplied in the
2671	  \(\Gamma \circ (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_{*}\) form and are spliced directly into the composite
2672	  by this congruence chaining.

2675	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_eUnit}) and meets its inverse inside the codomain
2676	  read. Cancellation~(2): the surviving pushforward-composition coherence has identity
2677	  \(\Gamma\)-image (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_pushforwardComp}, whose
2678	  section-level content is the collapse Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaMap_pushforwardComp_hom_eq_id}).

2680	  bakes in (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_pullbackComp}). The lone survivor is the
2681	  affine \((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)\)-unit, evaluated by Seam~1
2682	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_unit_value}) to \(\rho\).
2683	\end{proof}

2726	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex_legs}) at the projection legs
2727	  \(g' = \operatorname{pr}_1\), \(f' = \operatorname{pr}_2\). At those legs the concrete codomain

2735	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_unit_value}), read over \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) by restriction

2769	  is an \(R'\)-linear map \(R' \otimes_R M \to (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M\); on the generator
2770	  \(r' \otimes m\) it returns \(r' \cdot ((1 \otimes 1) \otimes m) = (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\),

2772	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}) on \(r' \otimes m\). Both maps being

2901	\begin{proof}
2902	  \uses{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read,
2903	        lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read, lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv,
2904	        lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso, lem:base_change_mate_gstar_counit_transport,
2905	        lem:base_change_mate_inner_value_eq,
2906	        lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex_legs,
2907	        lem:base_change_mate_gstar_generator_close}
2908	  This is the closing step of the affine section-level computation; the section
2909	  identity Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_section_identity} follows from it directly. The
2910	  argument is the counit dual of Seam~1, in four moves.
2911	
2912	  \emph{Counit factorization.} The base-change map
2913	  (Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}) is the \((g^* \dashv g_*)\)-transpose of
2914	  \(\theta_{\mathrm{in}}\) (\(g = \operatorname{Spec}\psi\)); by the counit form of the hom-set
2915	  adjunction it factors as \(g^*(\theta_{\mathrm{in}})\) followed by the counit \(\varepsilon_g\),
2916	  with no opaque transpose remaining,
2917	  \(\Gamma(\theta) = \Gamma\bigl(g^*(\theta_{\mathrm{in}})\bigr) \circ \Gamma(\varepsilon_g)\).
2918	  Splitting by functoriality of \(\Gamma\) and moving the domain read \(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}\),
2919	  the regrouping isomorphism, and the codomain read \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\) to the right leaves
2920	  on the left exactly the two geometric factors
2921	  \(\Gamma_{R'}\bigl(g^*(\theta_{\mathrm{in}})\bigr) \circ \Gamma_{R'}(\varepsilon_g)\). Because
2922	  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}\), \(\operatorname{regroup}\) and \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\) are themselves
2923	  conjugate-side comparisons, this splitting is performed on the conjugate side — applying injectivity
2924	  of the conjugation bijection and the reassoc conjugate coherences — rather than by a positional
2925	  head-symbol cancellation under the \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module instance diamond.
2926	
2927	  \emph{Counit transport (Seam~C).} By
2928	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_gstar_counit_transport}, conjugating the geometric counit
2929	  \(\Gamma_{R'}(\varepsilon_g)\) by the tilde/pullback dictionaries that \(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}\)
2930	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read}) and \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\)
2931	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}) bake in — the pullback dictionary
2932	  \(\Pi_{\psi}\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso} together with the tilde--\(\Gamma\)
2933	  counit — replaces it by the algebraic counit \(\varepsilon^{\mathrm{alg}}\) of the
2934	  extension/restriction-of-scalars adjunction along \(\psi\). The conjugated transpose becomes
2935	  \(\operatorname{ext}_\psi(\,\cdot\,) \circ \varepsilon^{\mathrm{alg}}\) applied to the
2936	  \(\operatorname{Spec} R\)-section reading of \(\theta_{\mathrm{in}}\).
2937	
2938	  \emph{Inner value (Seam~A).} The \(\operatorname{Spec} R\)-section reading of
2939	  \(\theta_{\mathrm{in}}\) is the canonical \(R\)-linear map
2940	  \(\rho : m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\) (Definition~\ref{def:base_change_mate_inner_value}),
2941	  recorded as Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_inner_value_eq}. This inner value is not obtained by a
2942	  direct section computation: it routes through the reindex
2943	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex_legs}, taken at the explicit composite leg and proved
2944	  on the conjugate side (Open Q2). With the inner value available the residual \(g^*\)-leg, conjugated
2945	  by \(\Pi_{\psi}\), is \(\operatorname{ext}_\psi(\rho)\).
2946	
2947	  \emph{Generator close (Seam~B).} By
2948	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_gstar_generator_close} the composite
2949	  \(\operatorname{ext}_\psi(\rho) \circ \varepsilon^{\mathrm{alg}}\) equals the inverse regrouping
2950	  isomorphism \(\operatorname{regroup}^{-1}\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}) — both
2951	  \(R'\)-linear and agreeing on \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\). This is the
2952	  claimed equation.
2953	\end{proof}

2957	%     reading it through the two tilde dictionaries pins it as the inverse of the regrouping
2958	%     isomorphism. ---

3039	  \[
3040	    \Theta_{\mathrm{src}}^{-1} \circ \Gamma(\theta) \circ \Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}
3041	      = \Theta_{\mathrm{src}}^{-1} \circ
3042	        \Gamma\bigl(g^*(\theta_{\mathrm{in}})\bigr) \circ \Gamma(\varepsilon_g) \circ
3043	        \Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}

3046	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_gstar_transpose}, which states it equals

3052	  flatness hypothesis used anywhere.

3187	  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}} \circ \Gamma(\alpha) \circ \Theta_{\mathrm{src}}^{-1}\) sends
3188	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m\). By the inverse formula of
3189	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cancelBaseChange_mathlib} this assignment is exactly
3190	  \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}^{-1}\); equivalently, in the
3191	  cancellation direction \((r' \otimes a) \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes (a \cdot m)\) is
3192	  \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\).
3193	
3194	  Since \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) is a Mathlib linear equivalence carrying
3195	  \emph{no} flatness hypothesis, the conjugated map is an isomorphism; being the
3196	  conjugate of \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) by the two dictionary isomorphisms
3197	  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}, \Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\), it follows that
3198	  \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is itself an isomorphism. This closes the affine case
3199	  \emph{directly} on global sections, with no separate abstract-mate-versus-concrete-map
3200	  identification at the level of sheaves. (The full equality
3201	  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}} \circ \Gamma(\alpha) \circ \Theta_{\mathrm{src}}^{-1}
3202	  = \operatorname{cancelBaseChange}^{-1}\) of the intended signature follows from the
3203	  same chain once \(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}, \Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\) are formed; the
3204	  formalized decl records the \(\mathrm{IsIso}\ \Gamma(\alpha)\) corollary, which is
3205	  what the affine close consumes.)

3208	\begin{lemma}\leanok
3209	[Affine base change]
3210	  \label{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}
3211	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso}
3212	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:base_change_map_affine_local,
3213	        lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens, lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso,
3214	        lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange,
3215	        lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
3216	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
3217	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I)
3218	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L906--918).
3219	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
3220	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
3221	  % Assume $f$ is affine.
3222	  % In this case $f_*\mathcal{F} \cong Rf_*\mathcal{F}$ is
3223	  % a quasi-coherent sheaf, and for every base change diagram
3224	  % (\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
3225	  % we have
3226	  % $$
3227	  % g^*f_*\mathcal{F} = f'_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}.
3228	  % $$"
3229	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
3230	  change''.}
3231	  Assume \(f : X \to S\) is an affine morphism and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a
3232	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Then for every base-change square as
3233	  above the canonical map of
3234	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an isomorphism
3235	  \[
3236	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \;\cong\; f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr).
3237	  \]
3238	  The quasi-coherence hypothesis on \(\mathcal{F}\) is essential and is not a mere
3239	  convenience: the proof works over an affine open by writing
3240	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) for a module \(M\), and a general
3241	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module need not be of this form
3242	  \(\widetilde{M}\); without it neither the affine description of the pushforward
3243	  nor the tensor-associativity computation below is available, and the conclusion
3244	  fails.
3245	\end{lemma}
3246	
3247	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The vanishing of higher direct images is
3248	% Lemma \ref{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing}.
3249	% The statement is local on $S$ and $S'$. Hence we may
3250	% assume $X = \Spec(A)$, $S = \Spec(R)$,
3251	% $S' = \Spec(R')$ and $\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}$
3252	% for some $A$-module $M$.
3253	% We use Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-widetilde-pullback}
3254	% to describe pullbacks and pushforwards of $\mathcal{F}$.
3255	% Namely, $X' = \Spec(R' \otimes_R A)$ and
3256	% $\mathcal{F}'$ is the quasi-coherent sheaf associated
3257	% to $(R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M$.
3258	% Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
3259	% equality
3260	% $$
3261	% (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
3262	% $$
3263	% as $R'$-modules."
3264	% NOTE: This affine lemma is closed directly on global sections, via the two named
3265	% obligations: lem:base_change_map_affine_local (locality reduction on the affine
3266	% opens of S') and lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange (the section-
3267	% level value of the base-change map is cancelBaseChange). Both proved affine tilde
3268	% dictionaries -- lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso and lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso --
3269	% feed the second obligation. cancelBaseChange is in Mathlib with no flatness; the
3270	% affine case carries no flatness hypothesis. No Čech infrastructure is used here.

3272	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens, lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso, lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso, lem:base_change_map_affine_local, lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange}
3273	  The proof is the composite of the two named obligations, taken \emph{directly on
3274	  global sections}.

3276	  \medskip
3277	  \emph{First reduction (locality on \(S'\)).} By
3278	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} it suffices to prove that the
3279	  base-change map of Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an
3280	  isomorphism on sections over every affine open of \(S'\). This is exactly the
3281	  locality reduction Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_map_affine_local}: restricting the
3282	  cartesian square to an affine open \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(R') \subseteq S'\) and
3283	  a chosen affine \(\operatorname{Spec}(R) \subseteq S\) containing \(g(U)\), the
3284	  hypothesis \([\mathrm{IsAffineHom}\ f]\) makes \(X\) restrict to
3285	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and quasi-coherence of \(\mathcal{F}\) makes it restrict
3286	  to \(\widetilde{M}\) for an \(A\)-module \(M\). (It is here that quasi-coherence is
3287	  used: it is what licenses writing \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\).) Computing the
3288	  fibre product gives \(X' = \operatorname{Spec}(R' \otimes_R A)\) and
3289	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) the tilde of the \((R' \otimes_R A)\)-module
3290	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\). The matter is thus reduced to the affine--affine
3291	  model, where the base-change map is a morphism \(\alpha\) of quasi-coherent
3292	  \((\operatorname{Spec} R')\)-modules.
3293	
3294	  \medskip
3295	  \emph{Passage to global sections.} The functor
3296	  \(\widetilde{(-)} : \operatorname{ModuleCat}(R') \to
3297	  (\operatorname{Spec} R').\mathrm{Modules}\) is fully faithful with essential image
3298	  the quasi-coherent modules; equivalently the counit
3299	  \(\widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal{N})} \to \mathcal{N}\) is an isomorphism exactly when
3300	  \(\mathcal{N}\) is quasi-coherent. Source and target of \(\alpha\) are
3301	  quasi-coherent, so both counits are isomorphisms and, by their naturality,
3302	  \[
3303	    \mathrm{IsIso}\,\alpha
3304	    \;\Longleftrightarrow\;
3305	    \mathrm{IsIso}\bigl(\Gamma(\alpha)\bigr),
3306	  \]
3307	  where \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is a concrete \(R'\)-linear map in
3308	  \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\). The whole computation therefore stays at the level
3309	  of modules.
3310	
3311	  \medskip
3312	  \emph{Section-level identification (the second obligation).} By the section-level
3313	  computation Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange}, the map
3314	  \(\Gamma(\alpha)\), with its domain \(\Gamma(g^*(f_*\widetilde{M}))\) identified with
3315	  \(R' \otimes_R M\) and its codomain \(\Gamma(f'_*(g')^*\widetilde{M})\) identified
3316	  with \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) through the two dictionaries
3317	  Lemmas~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso} and~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso},
3318	  \emph{is} the canonical cancellation isomorphism
3319	  \[
3320	    \operatorname{cancelBaseChange} :
3321	    (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M,
3322	    \qquad (r' \otimes a) \otimes m \;\mapsto\; r' \otimes (a \cdot m)
3323	  \]
3324	  of \(R'\)-modules (Lemma~\ref{lem:cancelBaseChange_mathlib}). This is the entire
3325	  content of the affine close, and it is performed \emph{directly on the module of
3326	  global sections} — there is no separate abstract adjoint-mate identification at the
3327	  level of sheaves. Because \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) is an isomorphism with
3328	  no flatness hypothesis whatsoever, \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is an isomorphism, hence so is
3329	  \(\alpha\) on the affine chart, and by the locality reduction so is the global
3330	  base-change map. Flatness of \(g\) plays no role in the affine lemma; it enters only
3331	  in the global statement Theorem~\ref{thm:flat_base_change_pushforward}, where it is

3335	\section{Flat base change for the pushforward}
3336	
3337	The global statement is assembled from the affine lemma and the single homological
3338	input that flatness preserves the finite equalizer computing \(H^0\). That input is
3339	supplied verbatim by Mathlib, recorded here as a dependency anchor.
3340	
3341	\begin{lemma}[Flat base change preserves finite equalizers]
3342	  \label{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
3343	  \lean{LinearMap.tensorEqLocusEquiv}
3344	  \mathlibok
3345	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
3346	  Let \(A \to B\) be a flat ring homomorphism (more generally, let \(B\) be a flat
3347	  \(A\)-module that is also an \(A\)-algebra, with the scalar-tower compatibility),
3348	  and let \(f, g : N \rightrightarrows P\) be a pair of \(A\)-linear maps. Write
3349	  \(\operatorname{eq}(f, g) = \{x \in N : f(x) = g(x)\}\) for their equalizer. Then the
3350	  canonical \(B\)-linear comparison map
3351	  \[
3352	    B \otimes_A \operatorname{eq}(f, g)
3353	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
3354	    \operatorname{eq}\bigl(\mathbf{1}_B \otimes f,\ \mathbf{1}_B \otimes g\bigr)
3355	  \]
3356	  is an isomorphism; that is, base change along a flat algebra commutes with the
3357	  formation of the finite equalizer (equivalently, of the kernel: the case
3358	  \(g = 0\) is \texttt{LinearMap.tensorKerEquiv}, underlain by
3359	  \texttt{Module.Flat.ker\_lTensor\_eq} and \texttt{Module.Flat.eqLocus\_lTensor\_eq}).
3360	  Since \(- \otimes_A B\) also preserves finite products, it follows that
3361	  \(- \otimes_A B\) carries the degree-\(0\)/\(1\) equalizer
3362	  \(\prod_i \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F}) \rightrightarrows
3363	    \prod_{i,j} \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F})\) of a finite cover to the corresponding
3364	  equalizer of the base-changed cover.
3365	\end{lemma}
3366	
3367	\begin{lemma}[Sheaf condition as an equalizer of products]
3368	  \label{lem:sheaf_equalizer_products_mathlib}
3369	  \lean{TopCat.Presheaf.isSheaf_iff_isSheafEqualizerProducts}
3370	  \mathlibok
3371	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
3372	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a presheaf with values in a category with products on a
3373	  topological space, and let \(\mathcal{U} = \{U_i\}_{i \in \iota}\) be a family of
3374	  opens with union \(U = \bigcup_i U_i\). Then \(\mathcal{F}\) satisfies the sheaf
3375	  condition on \(\mathcal{U}\) if and only if the fork
3376	  \[
3377	    \mathcal{F}(U)
3378	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\mathrm{res}\;}\;
3379	    \prod_i \mathcal{F}(U_i)
3380	    \;\underset{\mathrm{rightRes}}{\overset{\mathrm{leftRes}}{\rightrightarrows}}\;
3381	    \prod_{(i,j)} \mathcal{F}(U_i \cap U_j)
3382	  \]
3383	  is a limit (an equalizer), where the two parallel maps restrict the \(U_i\)- and
3384	  \(U_j\)-components to \(U_i \cap U_j\). This is Mathlib's
3385	  \texttt{isSheaf\_iff\_isSheafEqualizerProducts} together with the fork
3386	  \texttt{SheafConditionEqualizerProducts.fork}; it is exactly Čech degree
3387	  \(0 \to 1\) and requires no separatedness.
3388	\end{lemma}
3389	
3390	\begin{lemma}\leanok
3391	[Finite affine cover with quasi-compact overlaps]
3392	  \label{lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs}
3393	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.exists_finite_affineCover_inter_isQuasiCompact}
3394	  A quasi-compact scheme \(X\) admits a \emph{finite} affine open cover
3395	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\). If moreover \(X\) is
3396	  quasi-separated, then each pairwise intersection \(U_{ij} = U_i \cap U_j\) is
3397	  quasi-compact; and if \(X\) is separated, each \(U_{ij}\) is in fact affine.
3398	  \begin{proof}
3399	    The affine opens form a basis, so \(X\) is covered by affine opens; quasi-compactness
3400	    extracts a finite subcover \(U_1, \dots, U_t\) (by Mathlib's affine cover and the
3401	    finite-subcover property of a quasi-compact space). Quasi-separatedness says the
3402	    intersection of two quasi-compact opens is quasi-compact, so each \(U_{ij}\) is
3403	    quasi-compact. When \(X\) is separated the diagonal is a closed immersion, whence
3404	    the intersection of two affine opens is affine (Mathlib, for separated schemes).
3405	  \end{proof}
3406	\end{lemma}
3407	
3408	\begin{lemma}\leanok
3409	[$\Gamma(X,\mathcal{F})$ as a finite sheaf-condition equalizer]
3410	  \label{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer}
3411	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.gammaIsLimitSheafConditionFork}
3412	  % NOTE: the pinned Lean decl is strictly more general than this prose — it takes ANY
3413	  % open family `U : ι → X.Opens` over ANY scheme (no QCQS / finite / affine hypotheses), giving
3414	  % `IsLimit (fork M.presheaf U)`. The finite affine cover of a QCQS scheme is the intended
3415	  % application; the consolidated existential `Modules.exists_finite_affineCover_isLimit_sheafConditionFork`
3416	  % now has its own block, Lemma~\ref{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer_cover}.
3417	  \uses{lem:sheaf_equalizer_products_mathlib, lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs}
3418	  Let \(X\) be a quasi-compact, quasi-separated scheme and \(\mathcal{F}\) a sheaf of
3419	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Fix a finite affine cover \(\mathcal{U} = \{U_i\}_{1 \le i \le t}\)
3420	  of \(X\) as in Lemma~\ref{lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs}. Then the module of global
3421	  sections is the equalizer of the two restriction maps of the cover,
3422	  \[
3423	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})
3424	    \;=\;
3425	    \operatorname{eq}\Bigl(
3426	      \textstyle\prod_{i} \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F})
3427	      \;\rightrightarrows\;
3428	      \textstyle\prod_{i,j} \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F})
3429	    \Bigr),
3430	    \qquad
3431	    s \mapsto (s_i|_{U_{ij}}), \quad s \mapsto (s_j|_{U_{ij}}),
3432	  \]
3433	  and the products are \emph{finite} (indexed by the finite sets \(\{i\}\) and
3434	  \(\{(i,j)\}\)).
3435	  \begin{proof}
3436	    \uses{lem:sheaf_equalizer_products_mathlib, lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs}
3437	    Apply the equalizer-products form of the sheaf condition
3438	    (Lemma~\ref{lem:sheaf_equalizer_products_mathlib}) to the underlying sheaf of
3439	    \(\mathcal{F}\) and the cover \(\mathcal{U}\): since \(\bigcup_i U_i = X\), the
3440	    fork displayed there computes \(\mathcal{F}(X) = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\) as the
3441	    equalizer of \(\prod_i \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F}) \rightrightarrows \prod_{i,j}
3442	    \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F})\). The cover is finite by
3443	    Lemma~\ref{lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs}, so both products are finite. The
3444	    module structure is inherited objectwise: the forgetful functor to abelian
3445	    groups preserves and reflects the limit, so the equalizer is computed in
3446	    \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}\) over the ground ring.
3447	  \end{proof}
3448	\end{lemma}
3449	
3450	\begin{lemma}\leanok
3451	[Global sections as a finite equalizer over a finite affine cover]
3452	  \label{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer_cover}
3453	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.exists_finite_affineCover_isLimit_sheafConditionFork}
3454	  \uses{lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs, lem:gamma_finite_equalizer}
3455	  Let \(X\) be a quasi-compact, quasi-separated scheme and \(M : X.\mathrm{Modules}\). Then there
3456	  \emph{exists} a finite affine open cover \(\{U_i\}_{i \in \iota}\) of \(X\) — \(\iota\) finite, each
3457	  \(U_i\) affine, \(\bigsqcup_i U_i = \top\), all pairwise intersections \(U_i \cap U_j\)
3458	  quasi-compact — whose sheaf-condition fork of the underlying presheaf of \(M\) is a limit:
3459	  \[
3460	    \Gamma(X, M) \;=\; \operatorname{eq}\Bigl(
3461	      \textstyle\prod_i \Gamma(U_i, M) \;\rightrightarrows\; \prod_{i,j} \Gamma(U_{ij}, M)
3462	    \Bigr).
3463	  \]
3464	  This consolidates the finite affine cover Lemma~\ref{lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs} with the
3465	  sheaf-condition equalizer Lemma~\ref{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer} into a single existential, the
3466	  directly-usable ``global sections as a finite equalizer'' input of the flat-base-change argument.
3467	  \begin{proof}
3468	    \uses{lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs, lem:gamma_finite_equalizer}
3469	    Extract a finite affine cover with quasi-compact overlaps by
3470	    Lemma~\ref{lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs}, and apply the sheaf-condition equalizer
3471	    Lemma~\ref{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer} (in its form valid for any open family) to that cover; the
3472	    pair witnesses the existential.
3473	  \end{proof}
3474	\end{lemma}
3475	
3476	% NOTE: the former "A-linear equalizer presentation (FBC-B build-ahead)" subsection
3477	% (3 blocks pinning gammaCoverRestrictScalars/gammaCoverResMapsALinear/gammaEqLocusIso) was RETIRED —
3478	% replaced by the element-level route blocks (gammaModA/gammaResA/leftRes/rightRes/
3479	% gammaTopEquivEqLocus/baseChangeGammaEquiv) in the FBC-B globalization subsection. The retired pins
3480	% named never-built decls and created phantom frontier nodes.
3481	
3482	\begin{lemma}[Base change of the finite equalizer diagram]
3483	  \label{lem:base_changed_equalizer_diagram}
3484	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.baseChange_sheafConditionFork_tensorIso}
3485	  \uses{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer, lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}
3486	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 02KH ($i=0$ case), separated paragraph
3487	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.md; quoted verbatim in the
3488	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF of thm:flat_base_change_pushforward below).
3489	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If $\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \ldots \cup (U_t)_B$
3490	  % we obtain by base change, then it is still the case that each $(U_i)_B$
3491	  % is affine and that $X_B$ is separated. ... We have the following relation
3492	  % between the {\v C}ech complexes
3493	  % $\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) =
3494	  % \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B$
3495	  % as follows from Lemma \ref{lemma-affine-base-change}."
3496	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KH (``Flat base change''), $i=0$ case,
3497	  the relation \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}_B,\mathcal{F}_B) =
3498	  \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},\mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B\).}
3499	  Let \(A \to B\) be a ring map, \(X\) a separated scheme over \(A\) with finite
3500	  affine cover \(\mathcal{U} = \{U_i\}\) (so each \(U_{ij}\) is affine), and
3501	  \(\mathcal{F}\) quasi-coherent. Set \(X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec} A}
3502	  \operatorname{Spec} B\), \(\mathcal{F}_B\) the pullback, and
3503	  \(\mathcal{U}_B = \{(U_i)_B\}\). Then \(\mathcal{U}_B\) is a finite affine cover of
3504	  \(X_B\), and the sheaf-condition fork of Lemma~\ref{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer} for
3505	  \((X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\) is obtained from that for \((X, \mathcal{F})\) by applying
3506	  \(- \otimes_A B\): there are compatible isomorphisms
3507	  \[
3508	    \Gamma\bigl((U_i)_B, \mathcal{F}_B\bigr) \cong \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B,
3509	    \qquad
3510	    \Gamma\bigl((U_{ij})_B, \mathcal{F}_B\bigr) \cong \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B,
3511	  \]
3512	  intertwining the two restriction maps, so that the whole diagram
3513	  \(\prod_i \Gamma((U_i)_B,\mathcal{F}_B) \rightrightarrows \prod_{i,j}
3514	  \Gamma((U_{ij})_B,\mathcal{F}_B)\) is \((- \otimes_A B)\) applied to
3515	  \(\prod_i \Gamma(U_i,\mathcal{F}) \rightrightarrows \prod_{i,j}
3516	  \Gamma(U_{ij},\mathcal{F})\).
3517	  \begin{proof}
3518	    \uses{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer, lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}
3519	    Base change preserves affineness and open immersions, so \((U_i)_B\) is affine and
3520	    \(\{(U_i)_B\}\) covers \(X_B\); since \(X\) is separated each \(U_{ij}\), hence each
3521	    \((U_{ij})_B\), is affine, and the fork of
3522	    Lemma~\ref{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer} applies to \((X_B,\mathcal{F}_B)\). On each
3523	    affine term the global-sections form of the affine lemma
3524	    Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward} supplies the isomorphism
3525	    \(\Gamma((U_i)_B,\mathcal{F}_B) \cong \Gamma(U_i,\mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B\)
3526	    (and likewise on the \(U_{ij}\)); these are natural in restriction, so they
3527	    intertwine \(\mathrm{leftRes}\) and \(\mathrm{rightRes}\). Finally
3528	    \(- \otimes_A B\) commutes with the finite products \(\prod_i\), \(\prod_{i,j}\)
3529	    (Mathlib, tensor product preserves finite products), so the base-changed fork is
3530	    literally \((- \otimes_A B)\) applied to the original fork.
3531	  \end{proof}
3532	\end{lemma}

3534	\begin{lemma}[Flat base change, separated case]
3535	  \label{lem:flat_base_change_separated}
3536	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChange_pushforward_isIso_of_isSeparated}
3537	  \uses{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer, lem:base_changed_equalizer_diagram,
3538	        lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
3539	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 02KH ($i=0$ case), separated paragraph
3540	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.md; quoted verbatim in the
3541	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF of thm:flat_base_change_pushforward below).
3542	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Since $A \to B$ is flat, the same thing remains true on
3543	  % taking cohomology."
3544	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KH (``Flat base change''), $i=0$ case,
3545	  separated paragraph.}
3546	  Let \(A \to B\) be flat, \(X\) a separated, quasi-compact scheme over \(A\), and
3547	  \(\mathcal{F}\) quasi-coherent. Then the comparison map
3548	  \[
3549	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \;\longrightarrow\; \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
3550	  \]
3551	  is an isomorphism.
3552	  \begin{proof}
3553	    \uses{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer, lem:base_changed_equalizer_diagram,
3554	          lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
3555	    By Lemma~\ref{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer}, \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{F})\) and
3556	    \(\Gamma(X_B,\mathcal{F}_B)\) are the equalizers of the finite forks of the cover
3557	    \(\mathcal{U}\) and its base change \(\mathcal{U}_B\). By
3558	    Lemma~\ref{lem:base_changed_equalizer_diagram} the \(X_B\)-fork is the
3559	    \(X\)-fork with \(- \otimes_A B\) applied. Since \(A \to B\) is flat,
3560	    \(- \otimes_A B\) commutes with the formation of this finite equalizer
3561	    (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}); hence
3562	    \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B = \operatorname{eq}(\cdots) \otimes_A B
3563	    \xrightarrow{\sim} \operatorname{eq}(\cdots \otimes_A B) = \Gamma(X_B,\mathcal{F}_B)\),
3564	    and this iso is the comparison map.
3565	  \end{proof}
3566	\end{lemma}

3568	\begin{lemma}[Flat base change, Mayer--Vietoris reduction of the quasi-separated case]
3569	  \label{lem:flat_base_change_mayer_vietoris}
3570	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChange_pushforward_mayerVietoris}
3571	  \uses{lem:flat_base_change_separated, lem:affine_base_change_pushforward,
3572	        lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib, lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs}
3573	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 02KH ($i=0$ case), quasi-separated paragraph
3574	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.md; quoted verbatim in the
3575	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF of thm:flat_base_change_pushforward below).
3576	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "In case $X$ is quasi-separated, choose an affine open
3577	  % covering $\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \ldots \cup U_t$. ... The reader who
3578	  % wishes to avoid this spectral sequence can use Mayer-Vietoris and induction
3579	  % on $t$ as in the proof of Lemma
3580	  % \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images}."
3581	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KH (``Flat base change''), $i=0$ case,
3582	  quasi-separated paragraph (Mayer--Vietoris induction on \(t\)).}
3583	  Let \(A \to B\) be flat, \(X\) a quasi-compact, quasi-separated scheme over \(A\),
3584	  and \(\mathcal{F}\) quasi-coherent. Then the comparison map
3585	  \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \to \Gamma(X_B,\mathcal{F}_B)\) is an
3586	  isomorphism.
3587	  \begin{proof}
3588	    \uses{lem:flat_base_change_separated, lem:affine_base_change_pushforward,
3589	          lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib, lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs}
3590	    Choose a finite affine cover \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\)
3591	    (Lemma~\ref{lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs}) and induct on \(t\). For \(t = 1\),
3592	    \(X = U_1\) is affine and the claim is the affine lemma
3593	    Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}. For \(t > 1\) put \(V = U_t\)
3594	    (affine) and \(W = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_{t-1}\) (covered by \(t-1\) affines, hence
3595	    quasi-compact and quasi-separated). Their intersection \(W \cap V =
3596	    \bigcup_{i<t}(U_i \cap U_t)\) is covered by the \(t-1\) opens \(U_i \cap U_t\), each
3597	    an open of the affine \(U_i\) and so quasi-compact and separated; thus \(W \cap V\)
3598	    is separated and quasi-compact, and flat base change holds for it by the separated
3599	    case Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_base_change_separated}. The two-member cover
3600	    \(\{W, V\}\) of \(X\) gives the left-exact Mayer--Vietoris equalizer
3601	    \[
3602	      0 \to \Gamma(X,\mathcal{F}) \to
3603	        \Gamma(W,\mathcal{F}) \oplus \Gamma(V,\mathcal{F}) \to \Gamma(W\cap V,\mathcal{F}),
3604	    \]
3605	    and likewise for \(X_B\) with \(\{W_B, V_B\}\). Flatness makes \(- \otimes_A B\)
3606	    preserve this finite kernel/equalizer
3607	    (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}); since flat base change holds for
3608	    \(V\) (affine), for \(W\) (inductive hypothesis, \(t-1\) members), and for
3609	    \(W \cap V\) (separated), the five-lemma/equalizer comparison forces it for \(X\).
3610	  \end{proof}
3611	\end{lemma}

3614	  \label{lem:flat_base_change_reduce_global_sections}

3617	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 02KH (the reduction "part (1) follows from
3618	  % part (2)") (read from references/stacks-coherent.md; quoted verbatim in
3619	  % the SOURCE QUOTE PROOF of thm:flat_base_change_pushforward below).
3620	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "since $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is quasi-coherent ..., it is the

3623	  % corresponds to $- \otimes_A B$ on modules ... we see that it suffices to

3626	  ``part (1) follows from part (2)''.}
3627	  In the cartesian square with \(g\) flat and \(f\) quasi-compact and
3628	  quasi-separated, the sheaf-level base-change map
3629	  \(g^*(f_*\mathcal{F}) \to f'_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) of
3630	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an isomorphism if and only if,
3631	  after reducing to the affine bases \(S = \operatorname{Spec} A\),
3632	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec} B\), the module comparison map
3633	  \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \to \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\) is an
3634	  isomorphism.
3635	  \begin{proof}
3636	    \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
3637	    Being an isomorphism is local on \(S'\), so we may take \(S = \operatorname{Spec} A\)
3638	    and \(S' = \operatorname{Spec} B\) with \(A \to B\) flat. Because \(f\) is
3639	    quasi-compact and quasi-separated, \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent on \(S\)
3640	    (Stacks Tag 01XJ / Mathlib's quasi-coherence of the pushforward under a
3641	    quasi-compact quasi-separated morphism), hence is the sheaf associated to the
3642	    \(A\)-module \(\Gamma(S, f_*\mathcal{F}) = \Gamma(X,\mathcal{F})\); likewise
3643	    \(f'_*\mathcal{F}'\) is associated to \(\Gamma(X_B,\mathcal{F}_B)\). Pullback along
3644	    \(g\) corresponds to \(- \otimes_A B\) on modules, so under the
3645	    tilde-equivalence the sheaf base-change map is the tilde of the comparison map
3646	    \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \to \Gamma(X_B,\mathcal{F}_B)\); since
3647	    \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is fully faithful on quasi-coherent modules, one is an
3648	    isomorphism iff the other is.
3649	  \end{proof}
