3830	\subsection{The \texorpdfstring{$A$}{A}-module \texorpdfstring{$\operatorname{eqLocus}$}{eqLocus} globalization (FBC-B)}
3831	
3832	The separated-case argument is repackaged here at the level of \(A\)-modules, where
3833	\(A = \Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\) is the ring of global sections of the structure sheaf, so that flat
3834	base change becomes the Mathlib equivalence \(\operatorname{tensorEqLocusEquiv}\)
3835	(Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}) applied to an honest
3836	\(\operatorname{LinearMap.eqLocus}\). The blocks below construct that presentation concretely: every
3837	module of sections \(\Gamma(U, M)\) is viewed as an \(A\)-module by restriction of scalars along the
3838	structure-sheaf restriction \(A \to \Gamma(U,\mathcal{O}_X)\), the restriction maps of \(M\) become
3839	\(A\)-linear, and the two product legs of the sheaf condition assemble into a pair of \(A\)-linear maps
3840	whose equalizer locus is \(\Gamma(X, M)\). Throughout, \(M : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) is a sheaf of
3841	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules and \(U : \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)\) is an open cover with
3842	\(\bigvee_i U_i = \top\).
3843	
3844	The bijectivity of the keystone presentation rests on two element-level sheaf axioms of the underlying
3845	abelian-group sheaf \(M.\mathrm{presheaf}\), recorded as Mathlib dependency anchors.
3846	
3847	\begin{lemma}[Sheaf separatedness: local equality forces global equality]
3848	  \label{lem:sheaf_eq_of_locally_eq_mathlib}
3849	  \lean{TopCat.Sheaf.eq_of_locally_eq'}
3850	  \mathlibok
3851	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
3852	  Let \(\mathcal{G}\) be a sheaf on a topological space \(X\), \(U : \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)\) a
3853	  family covering an open \(V\) (i.e.\ \(V \le \bigvee_i U_i\)), and \(s, t \in \mathcal{G}(V)\) two
3854	  sections. If the restrictions of \(s\) and \(t\) to every \(U_i\) agree, then \(s = t\). This is the
3855	  separatedness half of the sheaf condition, stated element-wise.
3856	\end{lemma}
3857	
3858	\begin{lemma}[Sheaf gluing: a compatible family has a unique amalgamation]
3859	  \label{lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}
3860	  \lean{TopCat.Sheaf.existsUnique_gluing'}
3861	  \mathlibok
3862	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
3863	  Let \(\mathcal{G}\) be a sheaf on \(X\), \(U : \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)\) a family covering an open
3864	  \(V\), and \((s_i)_i\) a family of sections \(s_i \in \mathcal{G}(U_i)\) that is \emph{compatible}
3865	  (the restrictions of \(s_i\) and \(s_j\) to \(U_i \cap U_j\) agree for all \(i, j\)). Then there is a
3866	  unique \(s \in \mathcal{G}(V)\) whose restriction to each \(U_i\) is \(s_i\). This is the gluing half
3867	  of the sheaf condition, stated element-wise.
3868	\end{lemma}
3869	
3870	\begin{definition}[The ground ring of global sections]
3871	  \label{def:fbcb_groundRing}
3872	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.groundRing}
3873	  For a scheme \(X\), the \emph{ground ring} is the commutative ring
3874	  \(A = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X) = \mathcal{O}_X(\top)\) of global sections of the structure sheaf,
3875	  taken from the \(\mathbf{CommRing}\)-valued structure presheaf so its ring and algebra structures
3876	  resolve canonically.
3877	\end{definition}
3878	
3879	\begin{definition}[The structure-sheaf restriction ring map \texorpdfstring{$A \to \Gamma(U,\mathcal{O}_X)$}{A to sections over U}]
3880	  \label{def:fbcb_rhoU}
3881	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rhoU}
3882	  \uses{def:fbcb_groundRing}
3883	  For an open \(U \subseteq X\), the structure-sheaf restriction along \(U \subseteq \top\) is a ring
3884	  homomorphism \(\rho_U : A = \Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X) \to \Gamma(U,\mathcal{O}_X)\). It is the ring map
3885	  along which sections over \(U\) are regarded as \(A\)-modules.
3886	\end{definition}
3887	
3888	\begin{lemma}[Transitivity of the restriction ring maps]
3889	  \label{lem:fbcb_rhoU_comp}
3890	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rhoU_comp}
3891	  \uses{def:fbcb_rhoU}
3892	  For opens \(V \le U \subseteq X\), the structure-sheaf restriction
3893	  \(\Gamma(U,\mathcal{O}_X) \to \Gamma(V,\mathcal{O}_X)\) composed with \(\rho_U\) equals \(\rho_V\):
3894	  \[
3895	    \bigl(\Gamma(U,\mathcal{O}_X) \to \Gamma(V,\mathcal{O}_X)\bigr) \circ \rho_U = \rho_V .
3896	  \]
3897	  \begin{proof}
3898	    \uses{def:fbcb_rhoU}
3899	    Both sides are the structure-sheaf restriction \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X) \to \Gamma(V,\mathcal{O}_X)\)
3900	    along \(V \subseteq \top\), factored as \(V \subseteq U \subseteq \top\); functoriality of the
3901	    structure presheaf collapses the two-step restriction to the one-step restriction.
3902	  \end{proof}
3903	\end{lemma}
3904	
3905	\begin{definition}[Sections over \texorpdfstring{$U$}{U} as an \texorpdfstring{$A$}{A}-module]
3906	  \label{def:fbcb_gammaModA}
3907	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaModA}
3908	  \uses{def:fbcb_rhoU}
3909	  The sections \(\Gamma(U, M)\), a module over \(\Gamma(U,\mathcal{O}_X)\), are regarded as an
3910	  \(A\)-module by restriction of scalars along \(\rho_U\) (Definition~\ref{def:fbcb_rhoU}). This is the
3911	  common \(A\)-module home in which the global-sections equalizer is formed.
3912	\end{definition}
3913	
3914	\begin{definition}[The restriction \texorpdfstring{$\Gamma(U,M) \to \Gamma(V,M)$}{Gamma(U,M) to Gamma(V,M)} as an \texorpdfstring{$A$}{A}-module morphism]
3915	  \label{def:fbcb_gammaResAHom}
3916	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaResAHom}
3917	  \uses{def:fbcb_gammaModA, lem:fbcb_rhoU_comp}
3918	  For \(V \le U\), the structure-sheaf restriction \(M.\mathrm{map} : \Gamma(U,M) \to \Gamma(V,M)\) is
3919	  promoted to a morphism of \(A\)-modules \(\Gamma(U,M) \to \Gamma(V,M)\) (Definition~\ref{def:fbcb_gammaModA}).
3920	  It is built from \(M.\mathrm{map}\) by restriction of scalars, using the transitivity
3921	  \(\rho\)-coherence Lemma~\ref{lem:fbcb_rhoU_comp} to identify the two scalar actions.
3922	\end{definition}
3923	
3924	\begin{definition}[The \texorpdfstring{$A$}{A}-linear restriction map]
3925	  \label{def:fbcb_gammaResA}
3926	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaResA}
3927	  \uses{def:fbcb_gammaResAHom}
3928	  The underlying \(A\)-linear map \(\Gamma(U,M) \to_A \Gamma(V,M)\) of the \(A\)-module morphism of
3929	  Definition~\ref{def:fbcb_gammaResAHom}. It is the building block of the two product legs of the
3930	  equalizer.
3931	\end{definition}
3932	
3933	\begin{lemma}[The \texorpdfstring{$A$}{A}-linear restriction is the underlying presheaf restriction]
3934	  \label{lem:fbcb_gammaResA_apply}
3935	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaResA_apply}
3936	  \uses{def:fbcb_gammaResA}
3937	  On underlying elements, the \(A\)-linear restriction map of Definition~\ref{def:fbcb_gammaResA} acts
3938	  as the presheaf restriction of \(M\): for \(x \in \Gamma(U,M)\) and \(V \le U\),
3939	  \(\mathrm{gammaResA}(x) = (M.\mathrm{map})\,x\).
3940	  \begin{proof}
3941	    \uses{def:fbcb_gammaResA}
3942	    Restriction of scalars and the composition with the comparison isomorphism of
3943	    Definition~\ref{def:fbcb_gammaResAHom} leave the underlying function unchanged, so the map agrees
3944	    with \(M.\mathrm{map}\) on elements.
3945	  \end{proof}
3946	\end{lemma}
3947	
3948	\begin{lemma}[Functoriality of the \texorpdfstring{$A$}{A}-linear restriction maps]
3949	  \label{lem:fbcb_gammaResA_comp}
3950	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaResA_comp}
3951	  \uses{def:fbcb_gammaResA, lem:fbcb_gammaResA_apply}
3952	  For opens \(W \le V \le U\) and \(x \in \Gamma(U,M)\), the \(A\)-linear restrictions compose as the
3953	  single restriction:
3954	  \[
3955	    \mathrm{gammaResA}_{V \le U}\!\bigl(x\bigr) \text{ then } \mathrm{gammaResA}_{W \le V}
3956	      \;=\; \mathrm{gammaResA}_{W \le U}\!\bigl(x\bigr).
3957	  \]
3958	  \begin{proof}
3959	    \uses{def:fbcb_gammaResA, lem:fbcb_gammaResA_apply}
3960	    By Lemma~\ref{lem:fbcb_gammaResA_apply} each \(A\)-linear restriction is the underlying presheaf
3961	    restriction of \(M\); functoriality of the presheaf \(M\) collapses the composite of the two
3962	    restrictions \(W \subseteq V \subseteq U\) to the single restriction \(W \subseteq U\).
3963	  \end{proof}
3964	\end{lemma}
3965	
3966	\begin{definition}[The left restriction leg]
3967	  \label{def:fbcb_leftRes}
3968	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.leftRes}
3969	  \uses{def:fbcb_gammaResA}
3970	  The \(A\)-linear map
3971	  \[
3972	    \mathrm{leftRes} : \prod_i \Gamma(U_i, M) \longrightarrow \prod_{(i,j)} \Gamma(U_i \cap U_j, M),
3973	  \]
3974	  whose \((i,j)\)-component is the \(A\)-linear restriction \(\Gamma(U_i, M) \to \Gamma(U_i \cap U_j, M)\)
3975	  of Definition~\ref{def:fbcb_gammaResA} applied to the \(i\)-th factor (restriction along
3976	  \(U_i \cap U_j \subseteq U_i\)).
3977	\end{definition}
3978	
3979	\begin{definition}[The right restriction leg]
3980	  \label{def:fbcb_rightRes}
3981	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rightRes}
3982	  \uses{def:fbcb_gammaResA}
3983	  The \(A\)-linear map \(\mathrm{rightRes} : \prod_i \Gamma(U_i, M) \to \prod_{(i,j)} \Gamma(U_i \cap U_j, M)\)
3984	  whose \((i,j)\)-component is the \(A\)-linear restriction of Definition~\ref{def:fbcb_gammaResA} applied
3985	  to the \(j\)-th factor (restriction along \(U_i \cap U_j \subseteq U_j\)).
3986	\end{definition}
3987	
3988	\begin{definition}[Restriction of a global section to the cover]
3989	  \label{def:fbcb_toCover}
3990	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.toCover}
3991	  \uses{def:fbcb_gammaResA}
3992	  The \(A\)-linear map \(\mathrm{toCover} : \Gamma(\top, M) \to \prod_i \Gamma(U_i, M)\) restricting a
3993	  global section to each member of the cover, with \(i\)-th component the \(A\)-linear restriction
3994	  \(\Gamma(\top, M) \to \Gamma(U_i, M)\) of Definition~\ref{def:fbcb_gammaResA}.
3995	\end{definition}
3996	
3997	\begin{lemma}[The restricted family is compatible]
3998	  \label{lem:fbcb_leftRes_toCover}
3999	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.leftRes_toCover}
4000	  \uses{def:fbcb_leftRes, def:fbcb_rightRes, def:fbcb_toCover, lem:fbcb_gammaResA_comp}
4001	  For every global section \(s \in \Gamma(\top, M)\) the two legs agree on the restricted family:
4002	  \[
4003	    \mathrm{leftRes}\bigl(\mathrm{toCover}(s)\bigr) = \mathrm{rightRes}\bigl(\mathrm{toCover}(s)\bigr).
4004	  \]
4005	  \begin{proof}
4006	    \uses{def:fbcb_leftRes, def:fbcb_rightRes, def:fbcb_toCover, lem:fbcb_gammaResA_comp}
4007	    Both sides are, in the \((i,j)\)-component, the restriction of \(s\) from \(\top\) to
4008	    \(U_i \cap U_j\): the left leg restricts \(\top \to U_i \to U_i \cap U_j\) and the right leg
4009	    restricts \(\top \to U_j \to U_i \cap U_j\), and by the functoriality
4010	    Lemma~\ref{lem:fbcb_gammaResA_comp} each two-step restriction collapses to the one-step restriction
4011	    \(\top \to U_i \cap U_j\). Hence the two components coincide.
4012	  \end{proof}
4013	\end{lemma}
4014	
4015	\begin{definition}[The corestriction into the equalizer locus]
4016	  \label{def:fbcb_toCoverEqLocus}
4017	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.toCoverEqLocus}
4018	  \uses{def:fbcb_toCover, lem:fbcb_leftRes_toCover}
4019	  By the compatibility Lemma~\ref{lem:fbcb_leftRes_toCover}, the map \(\mathrm{toCover}\) corestricts to
4020	  an \(A\)-linear map
4021	  \[
4022	    \mathrm{toCoverEqLocus} : \Gamma(\top, M)
4023	      \longrightarrow \operatorname{eqLocus}(\mathrm{leftRes}, \mathrm{rightRes})
4024	      = \{\, t \in \textstyle\prod_i \Gamma(U_i,M) : \mathrm{leftRes}(t) = \mathrm{rightRes}(t)\,\}.
4025	  \]
4026	\end{definition}
4027	
4028	\begin{lemma}[Global sections as an \texorpdfstring{$A$}{A}-module equalizer locus]
4029	  \label{lem:fbcb_gammaTopEquivEqLocus}
4030	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaTopEquivEqLocus}
4031	  \uses{def:fbcb_toCoverEqLocus, def:fbcb_gammaModA, def:fbcb_gammaResA, def:fbcb_leftRes,
4032	        def:fbcb_rightRes, lem:sheaf_eq_of_locally_eq_mathlib, lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}
4033	  % NOTE: bijectivity is established by the ELEMENT-LEVEL sheaf axioms
4034	  % (lem:sheaf_eq_of_locally_eq_mathlib for injectivity, lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib for
4035	  % surjectivity) on the underlying Ab-sheaf M.presheaf; it does NOT route through the
4036	  % sheaf-condition-fork limit (lem:sheaf_equalizer_products_mathlib / gammaIsLimitSheafConditionFork).
4037	  For \(M : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) and any open cover \(U\) of \(X\) (\(\bigvee_i U_i = \top\)), the
4038	  corestriction \(\mathrm{toCoverEqLocus}\) of Definition~\ref{def:fbcb_toCoverEqLocus} is an
4039	  \(A\)-linear isomorphism
4040	  \[
4041	    \Gamma(X, M) = \Gamma(\top, M) \;\cong\; \operatorname{eqLocus}(\mathrm{leftRes}, \mathrm{rightRes}),
4042	  \]
4043	  identifying the global sections, as a module over the ground ring \(A = \Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\), with
4044	  the submodule on which the two restriction legs agree.
4045	  \begin{proof}
4046	    \uses{def:fbcb_toCoverEqLocus, lem:sheaf_eq_of_locally_eq_mathlib,
4047	          lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}
4048	    The map \(\mathrm{toCoverEqLocus}\) is \(A\)-linear by construction, so it suffices to prove it
4049	    bijective on underlying sets. \emph{Injectivity:} if two global sections \(s, t\) have equal images,
4050	    their restrictions to every \(U_i\) agree, so by sheaf separatedness
4051	    (Lemma~\ref{lem:sheaf_eq_of_locally_eq_mathlib}) applied to the underlying abelian-group sheaf
4052	    \(M.\mathrm{presheaf}\), \(s = t\). \emph{Surjectivity:} an element of the locus is a family
4053	    \((s_i)_i\) with \(\mathrm{leftRes} = \mathrm{rightRes}\), i.e.\ a compatible family on the cover; by
4054	    the gluing axiom (Lemma~\ref{lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}) it amalgamates to a global
4055	    section restricting to \((s_i)_i\). Hence \(\mathrm{toCoverEqLocus}\) is an \(A\)-linear bijection,
4056	    so an isomorphism.
4057	  \end{proof}
4058	\end{lemma}
4059	
4060	\begin{lemma}[Flat base change commutes with the \texorpdfstring{$H^0$}{H0} equalizer]
4061	  \label{lem:fbcb_baseChangeGammaEquiv}
4062	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.baseChangeGammaEquiv}
4063	  \uses{lem:fbcb_gammaTopEquivEqLocus, lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
4064	  Let \(B\) be a flat \(A\)-algebra (\(A = \Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)). Then base changing the global
4065	  sections along \(A \to B\) is the equalizer of the base-changed restriction legs: there is a
4066	  \(B\)-linear isomorphism
4067	  \[
4068	    B \otimes_A \Gamma(X, M) \;\cong\;
4069	      \operatorname{eqLocus}\bigl(\mathrm{id}_B \otimes \mathrm{leftRes},\ \mathrm{id}_B \otimes \mathrm{rightRes}\bigr).
4070	  \]
4071	  \begin{proof}
4072	    \uses{lem:fbcb_gammaTopEquivEqLocus, lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
4073	    Tensoring the keystone isomorphism Lemma~\ref{lem:fbcb_gammaTopEquivEqLocus} by \(B\) over \(A\)
4074	    gives \(B \otimes_A \Gamma(X,M) \cong B \otimes_A \operatorname{eqLocus}(\mathrm{leftRes},\mathrm{rightRes})\),
4075	    and by flatness of \(B\) over \(A\) the Mathlib equivalence \(\operatorname{tensorEqLocusEquiv}\)
4076	    (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}) carries \(B \otimes_A (-)\) of the equalizer to the
4077	    equalizer of the base-changed legs. Composing the two yields the stated \(B\)-linear isomorphism.
4078	    By the same presentation applied to the pulled-back sheaf, the right-hand side is the global sections
4079	    of \(M\) over the base-changed scheme, so this is the module-level core of the \(H^0\)
4080	    flat-base-change comparison.
4081	  \end{proof}
4082	\end{lemma}
4083

The directive claims these back already-landed Lean, but if they were actually landed, `sync_leanok` would have marked them — so either they're still pending or they're blueprint coverage for declarations that exist in Lean but haven't been synced yet.

- Looking at the FBC chapter more closely, some blocks like `lem:gamma_finite_equalizer` do have `\leanok`, while the FBC-B blocks don't. The directive says they back already-landed Lean with lower stakes, so the key point is that these are coverage blocks for formalized declarations, and whether `\leanok` is present depends on whether `sync_leanok` has run since the coverage writer added them.

3530	% --- (D) The section-localization descent: the gap1 keystone ---
3531	
3532	\begin{lemma}[Section-localization descent from a local-tilde cover (gap1 keystone, D)]
3533	  \label{lem:section_localization_descent}
3534	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isLocalizedModule_basicOpen_descent}
3535	  \uses{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_of_quasicoherent,
3536	    lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_cover_le_quasicoherentData, lem:existsUnique_gluing_mathlib,
3537	    lem:isLimitPullbackCone_mathlib, lem:isLocalization_flat_mathlib,
3538	    lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict}
3539	  % NOTE: the pinned Lean decl isLocalizedModule_basicOpen_descent does NOT yet exist; it is the
3540	  % genuine new content of gap1 and is reused by gap2's section-localization characterization.
3541	  % isLocalizedModule_basicOpen_of_presentation does NOT close it: that lemma needs a GLOBAL
3542	  % presentation of M, whereas here only per-D(r) tilde data is available.
3543	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", \S "Sections over principal opens",
3544	  % Lemma \texttt{lemma-invert-f-sections} (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2152-L2170).
3545	  % Cf. [Hartshorne], II.5.3.
3546	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
3547	  % "Let $X$ be a scheme. Let $f \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$.
3548	  %  Denote $X_f \subset X$ the open where $f$ is invertible, see
3549	  %  Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-f-open}.
3550	  %  If $X$ is quasi-compact and quasi-separated, the canonical map
3551	  %  $$
3552	  %  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)_f \longrightarrow \Gamma(X_f, \mathcal{O}_X)
3553	  %  $$
3554	  %  is an isomorphism. Moreover, if $\mathcal{F}$ is a quasi-coherent
3555	  %  sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules the map
3556	  %  $$
3557	  %  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})_f \longrightarrow \Gamma(X_f, \mathcal{F})
3558	  %  $$
3559	  %  is an isomorphism."
3560	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", \S ``Sections over principal opens'',
3561	  Lemma \texttt{lemma-invert-f-sections}; cf.\ [Hartshorne], II.5.3.}
3562	  Let \(M\) be a quasi-coherent sheaf of modules on \(\Spec R\) (equivalently, by
3563	  \cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_of_quasicoherent} and
3564	  \cref{lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_cover_le_quasicoherentData}, \(M\) is locally isomorphic to a
3565	  tilde \(\widetilde{N_j}\) on the members of a finite basic-open cover \(\{D(r_j)\}\) of
3566	  \(\Spec R\)). Then for every \(f \in R\) the section restriction
3567	  \(\Gamma(M, \top) \to \Gamma(M, D(f))\) exhibits the target as the localized module
3568	  \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}\,\Gamma(M, \top)\); that is, it is
3569	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\) over \(R\).
3570	\end{lemma}
3571	% NOTE: the lemma STATEMENT is cited to Stacks lemma-invert-f-sections / Hartshorne II.5.3; the proof
3572	% below is a project-internal descent (finite sheaf equalizer over the cover + flatness of
3573	% localization) that deliberately does NOT route through the global affine equivalence (which is
3574	% gap1 itself), so there is no transcribed `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF`.
3575	\begin{proof}
3576	  \uses{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_of_quasicoherent,
3577	    lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_cover_le_quasicoherentData, lem:existsUnique_gluing_mathlib,
3578	    lem:isLimitPullbackCone_mathlib, lem:isLocalization_flat_mathlib,
3579	    lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict}
3580	  Refine the quasi-coherence cover to a finite basic-open cover \(\{D(r_j)\}_{j=1}^m\) of \(\Spec R\)
3581	  with each \(D(r_j) \le q.X_{i(j)}\)
3582	  (\cref{lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_cover_le_quasicoherentData}). On each \(D(r_j)\) the restricted
3583	  counit is an isomorphism (\cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_of_quasicoherent}), so
3584	  \(M|_{D(r_j)} \cong \widetilde{N_j}\) with \(N_j = \Gamma(M, D(r_j))\) a module over \(R_{r_j}\),
3585	  and the basic-open sections of \(M\) below \(D(r_j)\) are the corresponding module localizations
3586	  (\cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict}).
3587	
3588	  Fix \(f \in R\). The sheaf condition for \(M\) over the finite cover \(\{D(r_j)\}\) and its pairwise
3589	  intersections presents the global sections as the finite equalizer
3590	  \[
3591	    \Gamma(M, \top) \;=\; \mathrm{eq}\Bigl(\textstyle\prod_j \Gamma(M, D(r_j))
3592	      \;\rightrightarrows\; \prod_{j,k} \Gamma(M, D(r_jr_k))\Bigr),
3593	  \]
3594	  the Mayer--Vietoris / unique-gluing description of sections
3595	  (\cref{lem:isLimitPullbackCone_mathlib}, \cref{lem:existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}); the same holds
3596	  with \(\top\) replaced by \(D(f)\) and the cover by \(\{D(fr_j)\}\). Localizing at
3597	  \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\) is exact and commutes with this finite limit because localization is flat
3598	  (\cref{lem:isLocalization_flat_mathlib}). On each cover piece the localization of
3599	  \(\Gamma(M, D(r_j))\) away from \(f\) is \(\Gamma(M, D(fr_j)) = \Gamma(M|_{D(r_j)}, D(f))\), a
3600	  localization of the tilde \(\widetilde{N_j}\) computed by
3601	  \cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict}; the same on the overlaps. Hence the localized equalizer
3602	  computes \(\Gamma(M, D(f))\), and the comparison map
3603	  \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}\,\Gamma(M, \top) \to \Gamma(M, D(f))\) is an isomorphism: the section
3604	  restriction is \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\) over \(R\). This is the
3605	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})_f \cong \Gamma(X_f, \mathcal{F})\) of Stacks
3606	  \texttt{lemma-invert-f-sections} on \(X = \Spec R\), \(X_f = D(f)\), obtained directly from
3607	  local-tilde data without appeal to the global affine equivalence.
3608	\end{proof}

3610	\begin{lemma}[Quasi-coherent on an affine has trivial $\widetilde{(-)}$-counit (gap1)]
3611	  \label{lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ}
3612	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isQuasicoherent}
3613	  \uses{lem:section_localization_descent, lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_iff_isLocalizedModule_restrict}
3614	  % NOTE: the Lean decl does NOT yet exist. gap1 is the PRIMARY stated gap of the Quot-foundations
3615	  % layer — the QCoh(Spec R) ≃ Mod R essential-image gap (Stacks lemma-quasi-coherent-affine). The
3616	  % assembly is now a one-liner: the genuine content lives in the section-localization descent

3620	  % per-element local-tilde transport (\cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_of_quasicoherent}, P1).

3623	  % (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L1279--1284).
3624	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X) = (\Spec(R), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)})$
3625	  % be an affine scheme. Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module. Then

16	\section{Setup and motivation}
17	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
18	
19	Generic flatness is the foundational input to the flattening-stratification
20	construction underlying Grothendieck's existence theorem for the Quot scheme
21	(\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S4--\S5).  Its content is that flatness of a
22	coherent sheaf is a generic property over an integral base: over a noetherian
23	integral scheme \(S\), a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module on a finite-type morphism
24	\(X \to S\) becomes flat after restricting to a non-empty open subscheme of
25	\(S\). Algebraically this is the statement that a finite module over a
26	finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain becomes free after inverting a
27	single non-zero element of the base ring.
28	
29	We work throughout with a noetherian (integral, where required) base scheme
30	\(S\), a finite-type morphism \(p : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
31	\(\F\).

34	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
35	
36	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
37	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
38	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
39	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
40	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
41	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
42	non-zero element of the base ring.

The FlatteningStratification chapter has 0 `\leanok` markers and many `\lean{}` hints plus some `\mathlibok` anchors. The `NOTE (iter-022): CLOSED` comment on `thm:generic_flatness_algebraic` suggests this was closed in iter-022, but `\leanok` isn't present. This is interesting.

Wait — the issue is that `\leanok` is managed by `sync_leanok`, and the FlatteningStratification chapter may have the `leanok` markers NOT present in the LaTeX file because `sync_leanok` didn't pick them up (the decls may use `sorry` still, or the sync wasn't run, or the chapter uses a different mechanism).
So `\leanok` gets added by sync when a sorry is resolved. If there are zero markers, either the sync hasn't run on this file or the Lean declarations still contain sorries. The git status shows an unstaged change to the FlatteningStratification.lean file, which could indicate new sorries were added. The `CLOSED` comment is puzzling given this, so I should check the actual Lean file to see what's going on.

2411	
2412	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2413	[Annihilator of a finitely generated module commutes with
2414	  localization]
2415	  \label{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map}
2416	  \lean{Module.annihilator_isLocalizedModule_eq_map}
2417	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(S \subseteq R\) be a multiplicative
2418	  submonoid, and let \(R_S = S^{-1}R\) be the localization of \(R\) at \(S\). Let
2419	  \(M\) be a \emph{finitely generated} \(R\)-module and let \(f : M \to M_S\) be
2420	  an \(R\)-linear map exhibiting \(M_S\) as the localized module \(S^{-1}M\)
2421	  (i.e.\ \(f\) is a localization of \(M\) at \(S\)). Then the annihilator of
2422	  \(M_S\) over \(R_S\) is the extension of the annihilator of \(M\) along the
2423	  structure map \(R \to R_S\):
2424	  \[
2425	    \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)
2426	    \;=\; (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S
2427	    \;=\; \mathrm{map}\bigl(R \to R_S\bigr)\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}_R M\bigr).
2428	  \]
2429	  Equivalently, for finitely generated \(M\) the annihilator commutes with
2430	  localization: \(\mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M) = S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M)\).
2431	
2432	  \emph{The finite-generation hypothesis is essential.} For a module that is not
2433	  finitely generated the inclusion \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M) \subseteq
2434	  \mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M)\) can be strict, so the identity fails. This is the
2435	  standard commutative-algebra fact that the annihilator of a finite module
2436	  localizes; it is recorded here as the load-bearing algebra engine for the
2437	  basic-open coherence of \cref{def:modules_annihilator}.
2438	\end{lemma}
2439	
2440	\begin{proof}
2441	  \(\supseteq\): if \(r \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\), then \(r\) kills every element of
2442	  \(M\), hence its image \(r/1 \in R_S\) kills every element of \(M_S\) (each such
2443	  element is \(m/s = (1/s)\cdot f(m)\) for some \(m \in M\), \(s \in S\), and
2444	  \((r/1)\cdot(m/s) = (1/s)\cdot f(r\,m) = 0\)). Thus
2445	  \((\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\).
2446	
2447	  \(\subseteq\): write \(M = \langle m_1, \dots, m_n\rangle\) using the
2448	  finite-generation hypothesis, and let \(y \in \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\). Write
2449	  \(y = a/s\) with \(a \in R\), \(s \in S\). For each generator \(m_i\) the element
2450	  \(y\cdot f(m_i) = (a/s)\cdot f(m_i) = 0\) in \(M_S\); by the definition of the
2451	  localized module this means there is \(u_i \in S\) with \(u_i\,a\,m_i = 0\) in
2452	  \(M\). Put \(u = \prod_{i=1}^n u_i \in S\). Then \(u\,a\) kills every generator
2453	  \(m_i\), hence kills all of \(M\), so \(u\,a \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\). Finally
2454	  \(y = a/s = (u\,a)/(u\,s)\) lies in \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}_R M =
2455	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\). Therefore \(\mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S) \subseteq
2456	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\), and combined with the reverse inclusion the two
2457	  ideals coincide. The use of a single common multiplier \(u = \prod_i u_i\) is
2458	  exactly the step that requires finitely many generators.
2459	\end{proof}

### `Picard_QuotScheme.tex`

#### High-Stakes: `lem:section_localization_descent` (keystone D)

**Label**: `lem:section_localization_descent`  
**Lean decl**: `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isLocalizedModule_basicOpen_descent` (does NOT yet exist — prover builds)  
**Source**: Stacks `lemma-invert-f-sections` (stacks-properties.tex L2152–2170, verbatim quoted); cf. Hartshorne II.5.3 ✓

**Statement faithfulness**: The Stacks source quotes "Γ(X,F)_f → Γ(X_f,F) is an isomorphism for quasi-coherent F on quasi-compact quasi-separated X." The blueprint statement matches this exactly on X = Spec R, X_f = D(f), rendering it as `IsLocalizedModule(powers f)` over R. ✓

**Proof sketch**: Detailed, complete, formalizable in 7 logical steps:
1. Refine the quasi-coherence cover to a finite basic-open cover {D(r_j)} with local-tilde data using `lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_cover_le_quasicoherentData` (**leanok** ✓)
2. On each D(r_j), the restricted counit is an iso by `lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_of_quasicoherent` (**leanok** ✓)
3. From that iso, `lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict` (**leanok** ✓) supplies the tilde localization on sub-basic-opens
4. Present Γ(M,⊤) as finite equalizer over {D(r_j)} using `lem:isLimitPullbackCone_mathlib` + `lem:existsUnique_gluing_mathlib` (**mathlibok** ✓)
5. Same for Γ(M,D(f)) over {D(fr_j)}
6. Localization is flat → commutes with finite equalizer: `lem:isLocalization_flat_mathlib` (**mathlibok** ✓)
7. On each piece the localized sections equal Γ(M,D(fr_j)) by `lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict`; conclude IsLocalizedModule

The key proof-internal choice (NOT using the global affine equivalence gap1 — which is itself the downstream consequence) is explicitly noted. This avoids circularity. ✓

**`\uses{}` satisfiability**:
- `lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_of_quasicoherent` — `\leanok` ✓
- `lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_cover_le_quasicoherentData` — `\leanok` ✓

- `lem:isLimitPullbackCone_mathlib` — `\mathlibok` ✓

### `Picard_GrassmannianCells.tex`

#### High-Stakes: `lem:gr_proper` (properness keystone)

**Label**: `lem:gr_proper`  
**Lean decl**: `AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isProper` (does NOT yet exist — prover builds)  
**Source**: Nitsure §1 "Properness" (read from source, verbatim quoted) ✓  
**`\uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, lem:gr_separated}`** — both `\leanok` ✓

**Proof sketch soundness** (DVR valuative criterion):

*Existence*: Given φ: Spec K → Gr(r,d) landing in chart U^I:
1. Choose index J minimizing ν(f(P^I_J)) over all r×r minors P^I_J of the matrix
2. Define g: Z[X^J] → K via g(X^J) = f((X^I_J)^{-1} · X^I) — sends generators to matrix entries expressed in the J-chart
3. Show all entries have ν ≥ 0: any other r×r minor P^J_K = (cofactor of P^I_J in P^I_K) · (P^I_J)^{-1} P^I_K, and by minimality ν(P^I_K) ≥ ν(P^I_J), so entries of X^J have ν ≥ 0
4. Factor g through R ⊂ K: since all g(X^J_ij) ∈ R, define SpecR → U^J → Gr(r,d)

*Uniqueness*: Two fillers restrict to φ on generic point (Spec K), agree there; by separatedness (`lem:gr_separated`, **leanok** ✓) they agree on SpecR.

The proof sketch is detailed and complete at the level a Lean prover can follow. The cofactor-expansion bound and the minimality argument are spelled out explicitly. The chart selection is concrete (which open to use is determined by the minimal valuation). ✓

**Additionally uses `def:gr_transition`** in proof `\uses` (beyond the statement `\uses`): the proof `\uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, lem:gr_separated, def:gr_transition}` — `def:gr_transition` has `\leanok` ✓.
